original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
Ferris Bueller, a popular high school senior in suburban Chicago, decides to skip school by faking an illness so his parents, Tom and Katie Bueller, will keep him home. He rigs his bedroom with a mannequin, a trophy, and a record player to sell the ruse. Ferris cajoles his anxious best friend Cameron Frye and girlfriend Sloane Peterson to join him in taking Cameron's father's restored 1961 Ferrari 250 GT California into downtown Chicago for the day. Ferris also engineers a phone call impersonation—Cameron posing as Sloane's father—to get Sloane excused from school for a fabricated family death.

At school, Ferris's absence quickly becomes legendary; donations and concern flood the school. Only Ferris's resentful sister Jeanie Bueller and the school's dean of students, Edward Rooney, suspect the truth. Rooney, convinced Ferris is playing hooky again, leaves campus to hunt him down, visiting local hangouts and finally driving to the Bueller house, where he tries to force entry. Rooney gets stuck in the mud, loses a shoe and his wallet, and is chased by the family dog; Jeanie returns home, mistakes Rooney for a burglar, kicks him, and calls the police. The police take Jeanie to the station for a prank call investigation where she briefly talks with a drug dealer, then is picked up by their mother.

Meanwhile, Ferris, Cameron, and Sloane leave the Ferrari with two garage attendants who promptly take it for a joyride, adding many miles to the odometer. The trio spend the day visiting Chicago landmarks: Wrigley Field to watch the Cubs, the Sears Tower, the Art Institute of Chicago (where Ferris delivers a quiet moment to Cameron in front of a painting), the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, and they join the Von Steuben Day parade. Ferris lip-syncs Wayne Newton's "Danke Schoen" and later leads a crowd in a raucous performance of the Beatles' "Twist and Shout." Ferris also poses as Abe Frohman, the sausage king of Chicago, to get an upscale table at a Rush Street restaurant, narrowly avoiding running into his own father.

Cameron becomes increasingly distraught over the Ferrari's odometer, fearing his abusive father's reaction. On the drive home they recover the car but discover hundreds of miles have been added. Ferris attempts to reverse the odometer by running the car in place in Cameron's father's garage, jacking the rear wheels up and spinning them backward. Cameron, in a burst of anger and a confrontation with his fear of his father, smashes the car's nose against the garage, accepts responsibility for his life, but then the car slips off the jack, rolls backwards through the garage glass and down a hillside ravine, totaling the Ferrari.

Ferris walks Sloane home and then races across suburban backyards toward his house, pursued by time. Rooney has returned to the Bueller home looking for evidence and waits at the front door. Jeanie, who has been trying to expose Ferris all day, unexpectedly arrives home first after speeding to beat him; she pretends to scold Ferris for trying to walk home sick, presents Rooney with his lost wallet to prove she 'saved' the day, and allows Rooney to drive the feigning Ferris to his house. Rooney again is harried by the dog and flees. Ferris makes it into bed just as his parents check on him; after they leave he breaks the fourth wall to tell the audience, "Life moves pretty fast. If you don't stop and look around once in a while, you could miss it." The film ends with Rooney humiliated and forced to catch a ride on a school bus, and Ferris addressing the audience again in a closing gag.
original_full_tokens.setting: Spring day, suburban Chicago and downtown Chicago

major_characters.name: Ferris Bueller
major_characters.description: Charismatic high school senior who engineers an elaborate day off.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To enjoy one perfect day of freedom before graduation and prove he can outwit authority.

major_characters.name: Cameron Frye
major_characters.description: Ferris's hypochondriacal best friend, son of an overbearing father, owner of the Ferrari.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To avoid confrontation with his father and escape his anxiety, ultimately forced to confront his fears.

major_characters.name: Sloane Peterson
major_characters.description: Ferris's girlfriend who joins the day in Chicago.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To spend a carefree, intimate day with Ferris before they go to separate colleges.

major_characters.name: Edward Rooney
major_characters.description: Dean of Students at Ferris's high school, obsessed with catching Ferris skipping school.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To expose Ferris as a truant and punish him to validate his own authority.

major_characters.name: Jeanie Bueller
major_characters.description: Ferris's resentful younger sister who wants to expose his antics.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To reveal Ferris's fraud out of sibling resentment and a desire for justice, later softening toward him.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: Young Judy Hopps in Bunnyburrow dreams of becoming a police officer in Zootopia despite bullying from Gideon Grey. Years later she graduates the Zootopia Police Academy as valedictorian but is assigned to parking duty by Chief Bogo. Judy tickets 200 cars in a day, then pursues Duke Weaselton for plant bulb theft and volunteers to investigate the case of 14 missing predators after Mrs. Otterton brings Emmitt Otterton's disappearance to the station. Assistant Mayor Bellwether authorizes Judy under the condition she solve it within 48 hours. A photo links Emmitt to con artist Nick Wilde, whom Judy coerces into helping by recording him confessing to tax fraud. Judy and Nick follow leads: the Oasis (meeting naturist yak Yax and elephant yoga instructor Nangi), a DMV run by sloths to trace a limo plate, and a limo owned by crime boss Mr. Big. Mr. Big reveals Otterton went "savage" and that he was last seen at the limo; Manchas, the chauffeur, later also goes savage and chases Judy and Nick until Manchas vanishes. Chief Bogo pressures Judy to resign, but she keeps investigating with Nick. Nick tells Judy his origin: bullied as a child scout, he became a con artist. Using traffic camera footage provided by Bellwether, Judy and Nick deduce wolves captured Manchas, but the wolves' van goes missing in a tunnel. They find the missing predators held at Cliffside Asylum, and evidence implicates Mayor Lionheart in hiding them; Judy transmits proof and Lionheart is arrested. Bellwether becomes mayor. At a press conference Judy speculates predators' biology causes savagery; her comment sparks citywide fear and predator discrimination, and Nick walks out. Judy, guilt-ridden, quits and returns to Bunnyburrow, where Gideon Grey apologizes and tells her that "night howlers" are a plant with a toxic psychotropic bulb. Judy returns to Zootopia, reconciles with Nick, and they trace the night howler bulbs to Duke Weaselton who points them to Doug, a ram chemist working in Bellwether's employ. Judy and Nick storm Doug's subterranean lab, seize a dart gun and serum, and flee through the subway pursued by Doug's henchmen. At the Natural History Museum Bellwether and two rams (Jesse and Woolter) intercept them; Bellwether reveals she engineered the predator panic to install a prey-dominated regime, using night howler-derived serum to make predators "go savage" and then framing the attacks to seize power. Bellwether fires a dart meant to make Nick attack Judy, but Nick had replaced the serum pellets with blueberries; Judy records Bellwether's confession on her recorder. Chief Bogo and ZPD arrive; Bellwether is arrested. Lionheart claims he hid the victims to protect Zootopia; an antidote is developed for the serum and the victims recover. Judy rejoins ZPD and recruits Nick as the first fox officer and her partner. The film ends with Judy and Nick pulling over Flash the sloth for speeding, establishing their partnership and restored public trust.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day Zootopia — a metropolis of anthropomorphic mammals

major_characters.name: Judy Hopps
major_characters.description: A rabbit from Bunnyburrow who becomes Zootopia's first bunny police officer.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Prove herself as a competent police officer and solve the missing-predator case to earn respect.

major_characters.name: Nick Wilde
major_characters.description: A sly con artist fox with a history of being bullied and hustling in Zootopia.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect himself and profit from cons while hiding vulnerability; eventually seeks trust and redemption through partnership with Judy.

major_characters.name: Dawn Bellwether
major_characters.description: Zootopia's assistant mayor (later mayor) who appears meek but masterminds a prey-supremacist conspiracy.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Incite fear of predators to consolidate political power and reshape Zootopia in favor of prey species.

major_characters.name: Leodore Lionheart
major_characters.description: The mayor of Zootopia who is arrested for hiding the savage predators at Cliffside Asylum.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect his reputation and the city by secretly isolating affected predators, claiming it was to prevent panic.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: Dewey Finn, guitarist for the failing band No Vacancy, is kicked out after his antics. Evicted roommate Ned Schneebly, pressured by his girlfriend Patty Di Marco, is offered a short substitute-teacher job at Horace Green Prep; Dewey impersonates Ned to take the gig to pay rent. At Horace Green Dewey introduces himself as "Mr. S.", ignores standard discipline, and discovers his fourth-grade class has musical talent (notably guitarist Zack Mooneyham, drummer Freddy Jones, pianist Lawrence, bassist Billy, lead singer Katie, and manager Summer Hathaway). Dewey secretly converts classroom time into rock lessons and rehearsals, teaching rock history and performance while hiding the band plan from Principal Rosalie Mullins and the parents. He bonds with Rosalie outside school by playing her favorite song and gaining her trust. Dewey enters the kids in the local Battle of the Bands to win $20,000 and reinstate himself in the music world; Summer negotiates the band's late entry by lying that the children are orphans with an illness so they can perform. The deception unravels when Ned receives a Horace Green paycheck, calls the school, and Dewey admits the impersonation to Ned; Patty exposes him at a parents' meeting and the police and Ned confront Dewey. Rosalie learns Dewey is an impostor and fires him. The students, determined to play, secretly go to the Battle of the Bands; Summer rallies them and convinces Dewey to come. Ned stands up to Patty and goes to the show. Onstage, the student band—now named School of Rock—performs an original song by Zack and wins the crowd despite losing the official contest to No Vacancy. Public pressure forces an encore; the band returns and plays AC/DC's "It's a Long Way to the Top (If You Wanna Rock 'n' Roll)". Afterwards Rosalie opens an after-school program called the School of Rock where Dewey coaches the band and Ned teaches beginner music, while the class continues performing together.
original_full_tokens.setting: Contemporary New York-area private prep school and local music venues

major_characters.name: Dewey Finn
major_characters.description: Arrogant, unemployed rock guitarist who impersonates a substitute teacher
major_characters.primary_motivations: Win Battle of the Bands to earn money and reclaim rock stardom by using the students as his band.

major_characters.name: Ned Schneebly
major_characters.description: Mild-mannered lifelong friend and the real substitute teacher
major_characters.primary_motivations: Keep Dewey from ruining both their lives and maintain stability under pressure from Patty.

major_characters.name: Rosalie Mullins
major_characters.description: Principal of Horace Green Prep
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect the school's standards and students, later support music when she sees its value.

major_characters.name: Summer Hathaway
major_characters.description: Resourceful student and the band's manager
major_characters.primary_motivations: Help the class perform and succeed while protecting the band's secrecy.

major_characters.name: Zack Mooneyham
major_characters.description: Shy student who becomes the band's lead guitarist and songwriter
major_characters.primary_motivations: Prove his musical talent and gain confidence through performing.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In a Scandinavian-styled kingdom of Arendelle, Princess Elsa (born with the power to create ice and snow) and her younger sister Anna play as children, conjuring a snowman they name Olaf. Elsa accidentally strikes Anna in the head with magic, turning some of Anna's hair white. Their parents, King Agnarr and Queen Iduna, take the girls to the trolls led by Grand Pabbie, who heals Anna and erases her memory of Elsa's magic, warning Elsa to control her powers. The royal family withdraws into isolation; Elsa is taught to suppress her emotions and wear gloves, and the sisters grow apart. After their parents die at sea, the castle gates are opened for Elsa's coronation when she turns 21. During the coronation celebrations Anna meets Prince Hans of the Southern Isles and impulsively becomes engaged to him that night. When Anna and Hans announce their engagement to Elsa, Elsa refuses and, in a confrontation, loses control; she exposes her powers to the crowd, creates an ice barrier, and flees across the fjord. In her flight she accidentally plunges Arendelle into an eternal winter. Accused of witchcraft by the Duke of Weselton, Elsa retreats to the North Mountain and builds an ice palace where, free from restraint, she discovers she can create without fear.

Anna volunteers to find Elsa and end the winter. She travels to the mountains, buys supplies at Wandering Oaken's, then hires ice-harvester Kristoff and his reindeer Sven to guide her. They are joined by Olaf, the living snowman Elsa inadvertently created. At Elsa's ice palace Anna begs her to return to Arendelle; during an emotional exchange Elsa accidentally strikes Anna in the heart with magic. Elsa panics, creates a giant snow guardian (Marshmallow) and drives them from the palace. Kristoff takes the wounded Anna to the trolls, who examine her and tell them Anna's heart is frozen; only an "act of true love" can thaw it. Kristoff races Anna back toward Arendelle, believing Hans can save her.

Back in Arendelle, the Duke organizes men to capture or kill Elsa. Hans and his captain reach Elsa's palace and subdue her; she is brought back to Arendelle, imprisoned in the dungeon, and publicly accused. Hans pretends to help Anna but when confronted by Anna pleading for a true-love's kiss, Hans reveals his plot: as a youngest son whose family holds no throne, he courted Anna to marry into power, intending to let Anna die and then kill Elsa to seize the throne. He plans to declare himself protector and rule Arendelle. He leaves Anna to die while he organizes a summary execution of Elsa.

Elsa escapes her shackles, flees into the blizzard on the fjord and, distraught, unwittingly intensifies the storm enveloping Arendelle. Kristoff races back toward the city. Hans confronts Elsa on the frozen fjord and raises a sword to kill her; Anna intervenes by throwing herself between them and is struck by Hans's blade, freezing solid in the act. Anna's sacrifice—an act of true love for her sister—thaws her frozen heart and breaks the curse. Elsa, realizing love can control her powers, dispels the winter and restores summer. She also conjures a permanent flurry above Olaf so he can exist in warmth. Hans is arrested and sent back to the Southern Isles; the Duke of Weselton is rebuked. Elsa reopens the castle gates, names Kristoff royal ice harvester/deliverer, and Anna and Kristoff's relationship is confirmed with a kiss. The sisters reconcile: Elsa promises never to isolate herself again and to rule openly, ending Arendelle's winter and restoring the kingdom.
original_full_tokens.setting: Early 19th-century–style Arendelle and the North Mountain

major_characters.name: Elsa
major_characters.description: Princess (later queen) of Arendelle with the power to create ice and snow
major_characters.primary_motivations: To control and hide her dangerous powers to protect others and avoid hurting those she loves.

major_characters.name: Anna
major_characters.description: Younger princess of Arendelle, impulsive and optimistic
major_characters.primary_motivations: To reunite with Elsa and end the eternal winter to save her kingdom and repair their relationship.

major_characters.name: Kristoff
major_characters.description: Rugged ice harvester who lives in the mountains with his reindeer Sven
major_characters.primary_motivations: To help Anna reach Elsa (and later to protect her), motivated by loyalty and growing love.

major_characters.name: Hans
major_characters.description: Prince of the Southern Isles who courts Anna
major_characters.primary_motivations: To gain power and a throne by marrying into Arendelle and removing Elsa and Anna when convenient.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: An elderly grandfather (the Grandfather) reads the book The Princess Bride to his sick grandson in their home. The book's story follows Buttercup, a farm girl in the kingdom of Florin, who loved her farmhand Westley; his stock phrase “as you wish” revealed his love. Westley leaves to seek fortune; Buttercup learns his ship was taken by the Dread Pirate Roberts and believes Westley dead. Buttercup is engaged to Prince Humperdinck of Florin but, while riding to clear her head, is kidnapped by a trio: Vizzini (a Sicilian strategist), Inigo Montoya (a skilled Spanish swordsman), and Fezzik (a huge strongman). They carry her by boat up the Cliffs of Insanity. A mysterious masked Man in Black pursues them, defeats Inigo in a courteous sword duel, knocks out Fezzik by choking him, and outwits Vizzini in a poisoned-wine battle of wits, killing Vizzini. The Man in Black reveals himself to Buttercup as the Dread Pirate Roberts; she pushes him off a cliff in fury, but he tumbles and says “as you wish,” revealing he is Westley. Westley explains he became Roberts’ successor rather than being killed. He and Buttercup flee toward the Fire Swamp; they survive R.O.U.S. (rodents of unusual size), lightning sand, and flame spurts, but are captured by Prince Humperdinck, who had arranged the kidnapping to manufacture a casus belli against the neighboring country Guilder. Humperdinck offers to free Westley if Buttercup will marry him; she agrees to save Westley, believing Westley will return for her. Instead, Humperdinck betrays Westley and hands him to his sadistic right-hand, Count Tyrone Rugen (the six-fingered man). Rugen tortures Westley in a subterranean Pit of Despair using a device called the Machine, leaving Westley seemingly dead. Meanwhile, Inigo, who has pursued the six-fingered man for twenty years to avenge his father Diego Montoya (whose killer was the six-fingered Count), and Fezzik — now working in Florin’s brute squad — learn Rugen is in the castle. Fezzik finds an inebriated Inigo in the Thieves' Forest; they reunite and agree they need the Man in Black’s planning. Inigo and Fezzik discover Westley’s body being hauled away by the albino. They take Westley to Miracle Max, a disgraced miracle-worker, who at first refuses but then, pressed by Valerie (Max’s wife) and the trio’s purpose (to ruin Humperdinck), makes a pill that brings Westley back — mostly dead but able to speak. Westley, Inigo and Fezzik plan a desperate assault on the castle during Buttercup’s wedding to Humperdinck. Disguised as the Dread Pirate Roberts (Fezzik in a dark cloak in a wheelbarrow, Inigo pushing, and Westley pale and nearly immobile), they bluff the castle guards, set the cloak on fire to cause panic, and gain entry. Inigo hunts Rugen through castle corridors; they fight in a banquet hall where Rugen wounds Inigo multiple times but Inigo ultimately kills Rugen after repeating his challenge: “Hello. My name is Inigo Montoya. You killed my father. Prepare to die.” Westley, feigning death earlier, frees himself and confronts Prince Humperdinck in the honeymoon suite. Humperdinck attempts to reassert control; Westley forces him to submit verbally and threatens to disfigure him rather than kill him. Buttercup, preparing to commit suicide rather than live married to Humperdinck, is reunited with Westley; she learns the wedding was rushed and not legal. Fezzik finds four white horses; the four (Westley, Buttercup, Inigo, Fezzik) escape on horseback. Back in the frame story, the grandson demands to hear the ending; the Grandfather reads the final romantic line and promises to return the next day, ending with “as you wish.”
original_full_tokens.setting: Medieval-style Florin and surrounding locales; present-day bedside frame

major_characters.name: Westley
major_characters.description: Former farmhand who becomes the Dread Pirate Roberts and Buttercup’s true love
major_characters.primary_motivations: Rescue and reunite with Buttercup because of his love for her, even at great risk.

major_characters.name: Buttercup
major_characters.description: Beautiful farm girl chosen to marry Prince Humperdinck; Westley’s love
major_characters.primary_motivations: Avoid marriage to Humperdinck and reunite with Westley because she loves him.

major_characters.name: Prince Humperdinck
major_characters.description: Odious prince of Florin who schemes to start a war and control Buttercup
major_characters.primary_motivations: Consolidate power and provoke war with Guilder by using Buttercup as a political pawn.

major_characters.name: Inigo Montoya
major_characters.description: Skilled swordsman seeking revenge for his father’s murder by a six-fingered man
major_characters.primary_motivations: Find and kill Count Rugen to avenge his father Diego Montoya.

major_characters.name: Fezzik
major_characters.description: Giant strongman, former henchman who befriends Westley and Inigo
major_characters.primary_motivations: Help his friends and use his strength to aid in rescuing Buttercup and fighting injustice.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In Santa Cecilia, Mexico, matriarch Imelda Rivera banned music after her husband (a musician) abandoned her and their infant daughter Coco; the family became shoemakers and maintained an ofrenda with ancestor photos. Ninety-six years later, 12-year-old Miguel Rivera secretly teaches himself guitar and idolizes film star musician Ernesto de la Cruz. On Día de los Muertos Miguel's abuelita destroys his homemade guitar; Miguel finds a torn photograph hidden behind a frame that shows a man holding Ernesto's famous guitar and assumes Ernesto is his great-great-grandfather. Miguel steals Ernesto's guitar from Ernesto's tomb in the municipal cemetery to enter a talent contest. When he strums it he becomes invisible to the living and is pulled into the Land of the Dead, a vast, colorful city accessed by a marigold-bridge. There the Riveras discover Imelda's photo is missing from the family's ofrenda in the living world, so Imelda cannot cross over. Miguel is told he has been cursed for stealing from the dead: he will become dead if he does not receive a blessing from a living family member using an aztec marigold petal before sunrise. Imelda offers a conditional blessing if Miguel renounces music; he accepts and returns to the living, then immediately steals Ernesto's guitar again and is transported back to the Land of the Dead. Miguel meets Hector, a scrappy skeleton who claims to have been a songwriter and Ernest o's former partner; Hector offers to help Miguel find Ernesto in exchange for Miguel returning Hector's photo to the living world so Hector's daughter will remember him. Hector guides Miguel to Ernesto's mansion. There Miguel learns—through Hector's accusations and later evidence—that Ernesto and Hector were once a duo, but Ernesto grew envious, poisoned Hector to keep him from leaving, stole Hector's songs and guitar, and passed them off as his own. Ernesto appears and welcomes Miguel as kin, then betrays them: Ernesto seizes Hector's photo and has Miguel and Hector thrown into a cenote. In the cenote Hector reveals he is Miguel's real great-great-grandfather and that his daughter is Coco; Coco (alive, elderly and increasingly forgetful) still remembers him. Dante, Miguel's stray dog who becomes an alebrije (spirit guide) in the Land of the Dead, helps rescue them. The dead Rivera family locates and infiltrates Ernesto's sunrise concert to recover Hector's photo and expose Ernesto. In the resulting confrontation, Ernesto is crushed by a falling bell just as in his recorded death, and Hector's photo is recovered but is damaged when it falls into water. As dawn approaches Hector begins to fade because Coco may forget him; Imelda, seeing Hector's truth and moved by reconciliation, gives Miguel an unconditional blessing using a marigold petal so he can return to the living. Miguel returns home, apologizes, and plays Hector's song "Remember Me" on the guitar; Coco sings and recounts her father's story, restoring Hector's memory. Miguel reunites with his family; the ban on music is lifted. One year later the family ofrenda includes Hector and the recently deceased Coco; letters prove Ernesto stole Hector's work and Ernesto's reputation is ruined in the living world while Hector receives posthumous recognition. In the Land of the Dead, Hector rejoins Imelda, Coco and the Rivera family, and Miguel performs for both worlds.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day Santa Cecilia, Mexico and the vibrant Land of the Dead

major_characters.name: Miguel Rivera
major_characters.description: A 12-year-old aspiring guitarist from the Rivera family who secretly loves music.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To become a musician and prove his identity when he believes Ernesto de la Cruz is his ancestor.

major_characters.name: Héctor
major_characters.description: A down-on-his-luck skeleton in the Land of the Dead who once performed with Ernesto and is mysteriously fading.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To get his photo returned to the living so his daughter Coco will remember him and he won't disappear.

major_characters.name: Imelda Rivera
major_characters.description: Rivera family matriarch who banned music after being abandoned by a musician husband.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To protect the family from the hurt she believes music caused, until the truth about Héctor changes her stance.

major_characters.name: Ernesto de la Cruz
major_characters.description: Famous deceased musician and film star idolized by Miguel, secretly a thief and murderer.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To preserve his fame and legacy by hiding that he stole Héctor's songs and murdered Héctor.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: Jesper Johansen, the spoiled, lazy son of the Royal Postmaster General, deliberately performs poorly at the postal academy. To force him to become responsible, his father ships him to the frozen island town of Smeerensburg with one year to post 6,000 letters or be cut off from the family. Jesper arrives to find a fogbound, hostile village dominated by two feuding families, the Ellingboes and the Krums; the post office is dilapidated and no one writes letters. The ferryman Mogens explains the town's coldness; teacher Alva (who now sells fish) tells Jesper the children don't attend school. Jesper initially plans to leave but, desperate for letters, studies an old map and locates a remote house belonging to a giant, reclusive toymaker named Klaus Klausson. When Jesper first meets Klaus, he is frightened by Klaus's size and solitary ways and flees, leaving behind a child's drawing of a home life. Klaus forces Jesper to deliver a toy from his house to the boy in the drawing; the boy's joy sparks other children to bring letters asking Klaus for toys. Jesper returns to Klaus and proposes a partnership: the toymaker will give toys and accompany Jesper on night deliveries; Klaus agrees if Jesper brings letters at night. Word spreads and more children learn to write so they can send letters; Alva reopens her school after using her savings to move back into teaching. Jesper invents a story that Klaus only gives toys to good children — a lie that motivates better behavior among the kids and, gradually, the adults. The Krums and Ellingboes begin to thaw when a Krums boy and an Ellingboes girl play together after receiving a toy, enraging their parents. Klaus reveals to Jesper that he and his late wife Lydia made toys for children they never had; seeing the community's joy, Klaus begins to make more toys. When the toy supply dwindles, Jesper, pushed by a Sami girl named Márgu who cannot speak Norwegian, persuades Klaus to help build a sled; Klaus resists at first but reconciles with Jesper and finishes the sled with him. The townspeople's generosity spreads; Jesper grows close to Alva and contemplates staying. The feuding family heads, Mr. Ellingboe and Mrs. Krum, resent the new goodwill and conspire to sabotage Jesper and Klaus. They manipulate Jesper's father into believing Jesper has posted 14,000 letters, prompting the postmaster general to visit Smeerensburg on Christmas Eve. The families plan to destroy Klaus's toys to rekindle the feud; during their attack Magda Ellingboe and Olaf Krum (their brawny children) fall in love in the chaos. Jesper tries to stop the destruction but appears to fail; Alva and Klaus, forewarned by the children, had already swapped real toys for wooden decoys in Klaus's sled. Jesper redeems himself through his efforts, and Klaus and Alva help deliver the real presents to the town. The public reveal of the children's letter-writing and the marriage between Magda and Olaf force the Ellingboes and Krums to end the feud. Jesper stays in Smeerensburg with his new purpose; he and Klaus continue delivering toys each Christmas and expand the tradition beyond the island for eleven years. Klaus gradually withdraws and then mysteriously disappears on the twelfth year; Jesper marries Alva, they raise two children, and Jesper continues to deliver gifts every Christmas Eve, waiting for Klaus's return as the town keeps the legend alive.
original_full_tokens.setting: Late 19th–early 20th century–style Smeerensburg, fogbound Arctic island

major_characters.name: Jesper Johansen
major_characters.description: Spoiled son of the Royal Postmaster General sent to Smeerensburg to learn responsibility.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Post 6,000 letters to avoid disinheritance and, ultimately, find purpose and redemption through community service.

major_characters.name: Klaus Klausson
major_characters.description: Reclusive, large toymaker living in a remote cabin full of handmade toys.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Keep the memory of his late wife alive by making toys for children and, reluctantly, help restore joy to the town.

major_characters.name: Alva
major_characters.description: Bitter schoolteacher-turned-fishmonger who reopens her school to teach children to read and write.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Restore education and hope in Smeerensburg and escape poverty, then stay to help rebuild the community.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: Young Carl Fredricksen idolizes explorer Charles F. Muntz and befriends tomboy Ellie, who dreams of moving their neighborhood clubhouse to a cliff overlooking Paradise Falls in Venezuela. Carl and Ellie marry, restore a house in the neighborhood, save toward a trip to Paradise Falls but repeatedly spend the money on necessities; Ellie dies after giving Carl her scrapbook and the two never make the trip. Decades later, 78-year-old widower Carl resists the neighborhood's redevelopment, injures a construction worker trying to protect his mailbox, and is ordered to move to a retirement home. To keep his promise to Ellie, Carl attaches thousands of helium balloons to his house and lifts it into the sky bound for Paradise Falls. Wilderness Explorer scout Russell, who came to Carl's doorstep seeking a merit badge for assisting the elderly, is accidentally carried along as a stowaway. During a storm the house lands on a high South American plateau opposite Paradise Falls where the balloons are losing lift. Carl and Russell explore the plateau, tethered to the buoyant house, and encounter a giant, colorful flightless bird (Russell names her Kevin) and a talking dog named Dug, whose collar translates his thoughts. A pack of dogs with tracking collars hunts the bird; their master is Charles Muntz, now a reclusive, obsessive adventurer living aboard a massive dirigible. Muntz initially greets Carl as a hero but quickly reveals he has become paranoid and violent in his quest to capture proof of Kevin. After Russell's friendship with Kevin is exposed, Muntz becomes hostile and imprisons Kevin on the zeppelin. Carl, Russell, Kevin and Dug escape and try to return Kevin to her chicks, but Muntz's dogs pursue them. Muntz's hunting party captures Kevin with a net; he burns under the tethered house to force Carl to choose between saving his home or Kevin. Carl attempts to save both; he retrieves Ellie's scrapbook and reads her final note, which urges him to have new adventures. Russell, determined, sails off on balloons to rescue Kevin and is captured by Muntz. Carl empties the house, follows in pursuit, boards Muntz's airship, and frees Russell and Kevin. A chase across the dirigible ends with Muntz lunging after them, snagging on balloon lines; he falls to his death. The house is torn free and descends out of sight; Carl accepts losing it. Kevin is reunited with her chicks in the jungle, Muntz's airship returns to the city with the animals, and Carl proudly presents Russell with his final Wilderness Explorer badge plus Ellie's grape-soda-bottle-cap “Ellie badge.” The film ends with Carl living a fuller life as a surrogate grandfather to Russell, and the house resting on the cliff beside Paradise Falls as Ellie had wanted.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day (present) South America—Paradise Falls; also U.S. city neighborhood

major_characters.name: Carl Fredricksen
major_characters.description: 78-year-old widowed balloon salesman determined to fulfill a promise to his late wife.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Keep the promise to Ellie by taking their house to Paradise Falls and preserve their life together.

major_characters.name: Russell
major_characters.description: Enthusiastic young Wilderness Explorer scout who becomes an accidental stowaway.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Earn his final merit badge by assisting an elderly person and prove himself.

major_characters.name: Charles F. Muntz
major_characters.description: Famous explorer turned obsessive recluse living on a dirigible, hunting a giant bird.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Recover definitive proof of the giant bird to restore his reputation at any cost.

major_characters.name: Kevin
major_characters.description: A rare, large, colorful flightless bird mother whose chicks are hidden in the jungle.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect and return to her chicks; her presence drives the pursuit and conflicts.

major_characters.name: Dug
major_characters.description: A friendly dog with a collar that translates his thoughts into speech; formerly Muntz's dog.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Be loyal to his new master (Carl) and help rescue Kevin and Russell.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
When Hun leader Shan Yu breaches China’s northern border, the Imperial court orders one man from each family to join the army. In a village, tomboyish Fa Mulan fails a matchmaker visit and fears bringing dishonor when her elderly, lame father Fa Zhou is conscripted. To spare him, Mulan cuts her hair, takes Fa Zhou’s armor and horse Khan, and leaves disguised as a son named “Ping.” Her grandmother’s ancestors debate her actions; the disgraced tiny dragon Mushu is sent (after accidentally destroying the stone guardian) to rouse the guardian but instead accompanies Mulan to redeem himself. Mulan joins basic training under Captain Li Shang alongside recruits Yao, Ling, and Chien-Po; initially clumsy, she gradually masters soldiering—helped by Mushu’s schemes—and earns the men’s respect. Chi-Fu, the emperor’s adviser, remains skeptical and later reports Li Shang’s troops unfit.
General Shang (General Li?) leads the army north but is ambushed; Li Shang pursues Shan Yu to a mountain pass. During the confrontation, Mulan rigs the last huolongchushui cannon to trigger an avalanche that buries the Hun army, apparently killing Shan Yu. Mulan is wounded in the battle; her gender is revealed when Li Shang treats her wound. Bound by military law, Li Shang spares her life but expels her; the company marches to the Imperial City to claim victory while Mulan is left behind. Mushu admits he lied about being sent by the ancestors; Cri-Kee admits he isn’t truly lucky.
Shan Yu survives and infiltrates the Imperial City, hiding among the returning troops and seizing the palace, taking the Emperor captive. Mulan sneaks into the city and warns Li Shang, who does not believe her; when the Huns seize the palace, Li Shang, Yao, Ling, and Chien-Po—now convinced—accept her plan. Mulan and the others infiltrate the palace (disguising most as concubines) and free the Emperor. Mulan lures Shan Yu onto the imperial roof and fights him; Mushu ignites a large firework that blasts Shan Yu off the roof and kills him. After the battle, Chi-Fu attempts to punish Mulan for impersonation, but the Emperor publicly honors her bravery, presents her with Shan Yu’s sword and a medallion, and offers her a position on his council. Mulan declines and returns home with the Emperor’s gifts. At the Fa household, Fa Zhou embraces Mulan rather than the trophies; Li Shang arrives, returns Mulan’s helmet, and accepts her invitation to stay for dinner. Mushu is restored as the Fa family guardian by the ancestors.
original_full_tokens.setting: Imperial China, rural village to Imperial City (ancient/medieval period)

major_characters.name: Fa Mulan
major_characters.description: A rebellious young woman who disguises herself as a male soldier to save her father and country.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect her father and restore her family’s honor by taking his place in the army and proving her worth.

major_characters.name: Captain Li Shang
major_characters.description: Commander of Mulan’s training unit and son of General Li (General Shigeta); disciplined army captain.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Train effective troops and defend the Emperor; he seeks military honor and to lead capable soldiers.

major_characters.name: Mushu
major_characters.description: A small disgraced family guardian dragon who chooses to help Mulan to regain status.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Restore his guardian status by ensuring Mulan becomes a hero, even if his motives are selfish at first.

major_characters.name: Shan Yu
major_characters.description: Ruthless leader of the Huns invading northern China.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Conquer China and overthrow the Emperor through force and intimidation.

major_characters.name: Fa Zhou
major_characters.description: Mulan’s elderly father and veteran with a leg wound, formerly honored in the family.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Uphold family honor by answering the conscription—even though age and injury make service deadly.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: Shrek, a reclusive green ogre living in a swamp, wakes to find his home overrun by banished fairy-tale creatures (Pinocchio, the Three Little Pigs, the Gingerbread Man, etc.) who were expelled from Lord Farquaad's kingdom. Angry, Shrek travels to Duloc with a talkative Donkey (who escaped the crowd) to confront Lord Farquaad and demand the creatures be returned. In Duloc Farquaad, seeking to become king, consults a magic mirror that points him to Princess Fiona as the bride he must marry. During a tournament to determine who will attempt Fiona's rescue from a lava-surrounded castle, Shrek defeats the competitors (with Donkey's help) and Farquaad strikes a bargain: Shrek will rescue Fiona in exchange for the return of his swamp. Shrek and Donkey travel to the castle; Donkey wins over the female Dragon and is kept safe in her chambers, while Shrek finds Fiona in the tower. Fiona is initially pleased but disappointed when she sees Shrek is an ogre. They escape when Dragon, angered by Donkey's removal, chases them out. On the journey back to Duloc, Shrek and Fiona grow closer and begin to fall in love. At night Fiona refuses to sleep near them; when Donkey investigates one night he discovers Fiona transforms into an ogress in a windmill. Fiona explains privately to Donkey that she is cursed: by day she is human, by night she becomes an ogress, and only a true love's kiss will break the spell. She makes Donkey promise not to tell Shrek. Shrek overhears part of Fiona's apology about her "ugly beast" form and, misunderstanding that she means him, withdraws in hurt. The next morning Shrek takes Fiona to Duloc and hands her over to Farquaad; he then returns to his now-cleared swamp and angrily severs his friendship with Donkey. Donkey, who kept Fiona's confidence, eventually convinces Shrek that Fiona's words were about herself, not Shrek, and that Fiona will soon marry Farquaad. Donkey and Shrek race to Duloc with Dragon (who fell for Donkey and followed them) and crash Fiona and Farquaad's wedding. As the sun sets before Farquaad can kiss Fiona, she transforms into an ogress in front of the crowd. Farquaad orders Shrek killed and imprisons Fiona; Shrek summons Dragon, who bursts into the wedding, eats Farquaad, and destroys Duloc's authority. Shrek and Fiona confess their love and kiss; the spell's light appears but Fiona remains an ogress—her "true form" turned out to be ogresshood. They realize they love each other as they are. The film ends with Shrek and Fiona's wedding in the swamp and a celebratory reception with Donkey and the fairy-tale creatures; Shrek and Fiona depart on their honeymoon.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern fairy-tale world (swamp, Duloc, lava-surrounded castle)

major_characters.name: Shrek
major_characters.description: A solitary, gruff green ogre who values his privacy.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Get his swamp back and preserve his solitude, then protect and be with Fiona once he falls in love.

major_characters.name: Donkey
major_characters.description: A talkative, cheerful donkey and refugee among the fairy-tale creatures.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Stick with Shrek and help him, forging a friendship and preventing Shrek from giving up on Fiona.

major_characters.name: Princess Fiona
major_characters.description: A princess locked in a tower by night-day curse that makes her an ogress after dark.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Find true love to break her curse and return to a safe, accepted life—while hiding her nightly transformation.

major_characters.name: Lord Farquaad
major_characters.description: Ruler of Duloc who banishes fairy-tale beings and seeks a princess to become king.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Marry a princess to legitimize his rule and create a perfect kingdom, regardless of others' suffering.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: At the North Pole, Santa Claus catches a severe cold; Dr. Flammer diagnoses him and advises he stay home, saying public belief in Santa is waning. Discouraged, Santa announces he will cancel Christmas and take a holiday. Mrs. Claus refuses to accept this and considers delivering gifts herself. She recruits two elves, Jingle and Jangle Bells, to find proof that people still care about Santa. The elves take Vixen and head to Southtown, USA, where it never snows and where they hope to find Christmas spirit. Santa, suspicious that Mrs. Claus has sent the elves, follows on Dasher. On the way south, Jingle and Jangle are intercepted by the Miser brothers: Snow Miser and Heat Miser. Vixen escapes with the elves. In Southtown the elves are stopped by a policeman, ticketed for riding a reindeer on a one-way street and for their attire; they disguise Vixen by covering her antlers, but a cat chases them and they climb a tree. The dogcatcher takes Vixen to the pound. The elves talk with several local children who claim they no longer need Santa and regard belief as childish. Santa, traveling incognito, meets Ignatius 'Iggy' Thistlewhite and, posing under a false name, learns that Iggy's family and his father do believe in Santa. Iggy helps Santa and the elves by locating Vixen in the pound; when they go to the mayor to demand her release the mayor refuses unless they can make it snow in Southtown. Santa rescues a sick Vixen from the pound and returns her to the North Pole on Dasher. Back at the North Pole, Mrs. Claus, the elves and Blitzen go to ask Snow Miser to make it snow in Southtown. Snow Miser says he lacks authority because Heat Miser controls Southtown; they confront Heat Miser, who refuses because of a grudge against Santa and demands control of the North Pole in exchange. Mrs. Claus threatens to appeal to the misers' mother, Mother Nature. Mother Nature summons both brothers and brokers a compromise: Snow Miser will give Southtown a snowstorm in exchange for one spring day at the North Pole. With the promise secured, Santa nurses Vixen back to health, places her in his bed, and falls asleep in his chair, still unconvinced. The mayor of Southtown wakes to find snow falling; the town and children worldwide react by declaring a holiday for Santa and sending him gifts and letters. A little girl's letter saying she'll have a 'blue Christmas' without him finally convinces Santa that people still care; he recovers his faith and decides to make his Christmas Eve deliveries after all.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1970s-era North Pole and Southtown, USA

major_characters.name: Santa Claus
major_characters.description: Weary, sick Santa who decides to cancel Christmas.
major_characters.primary_motivations: He retreats from his duties because he believes people no longer care about him or Christmas; he seeks rest but ultimately must decide whether to resume delivering gifts.

major_characters.name: Mrs. Claus
major_characters.description: Determined wife who refuses to let Christmas be canceled.
major_characters.primary_motivations: She aims to restore Santa's faith and save Christmas by proving the world still believes in him.

major_characters.name: Jingle and Jangle Bells
major_characters.description: Two eager, quirky elves who travel to Southtown with Vixen to find believers.
major_characters.primary_motivations: They want to demonstrate the existence of Christmas spirit to convince Santa to resume his duties.

major_characters.name: Snow Miser
major_characters.description: Personification of cold who controls snow and the winter climate.
major_characters.primary_motivations: He wants to maintain his domain but will help if a bargain or authority change is arranged.

major_characters.name: Heat Miser
major_characters.description: Personification of heat who controls warm weather and Southtown's climate.
major_characters.primary_motivations: He resents Santa and refuses to allow snow in Southtown unless he gains leverage over the North Pole.

major_characters.name: Mother Nature
major_characters.description: The misers' mother who mediates between Snow Miser and Heat Miser.
major_characters.primary_motivations: She seeks to resolve her sons' dispute by brokering a fair compromise that restores balance.

major_characters.name: Ignatius 'Iggy' Thistlewhite
major_characters.description: A Southtown boy whose family still believes in Santa and who helps rescue Vixen.
major_characters.primary_motivations: He wants to help save Vixen and prove that belief in Santa remains alive in his town.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
After civilization collapses into a desert wasteland, Max Rockatansky is captured by Immortan Joe's War Boys and taken to the Citadel, where his hair is shaved and vital information is tattooed on his back. Designated a "universal blood donor," Max is chained and used to transfuse blood to a sick War Boy named Nux. Imperator Furiosa, one of Joe's lieutenants with a mechanical arm, drives the armored War Rig out to barter with nearby allies (the Bullet Farm and Gas Town). Unbeknownst to most of Joe's men, Furiosa has loaded Joe's five breeding wives (Capable, Cheedo, Toast, The Dag, and Angharad) into the rig and escapes toward a childhood haven she calls the Green Place. When Joe notices his wives missing he launches a massive pursuit, calling up all forces including the War Boys led by his sons Rictus Erectus and the zealot Immortan Joe himself; Nux straps Max to his vehicle so Max accompanies the chase as a living blood bag.
Furiosa's crew fights multiple chases: first against Buzzards who attack in hostile territory, then into a sandstorm that separates pursuers. Nux tries to sacrifice himself to destroy the rig during the storm; Max frees himself, subdues Nux and, after the storm, finds Furiosa repairing the War Rig with the five wives. Max initially steals the rig but cannot fully control it and stays with the group. Nux sneaks aboard later; after the wives overpower him, he is captured by Joe's forces but later rescued emotionally by Capable and re-joins the fugitives.
They attempt to reach the Green Place but are intercepted by a biker clan in a canyon; the bikers try to block Joe with detonations. Joe forces his way through with a massive vehicle, and during the ensuing confrontation Angharad—heavily pregnant and Joe's favorite—falls, is crushed by Joe's car, and dies with her unborn child. Furiosa reveals the Green Place was a childhood memory; later, the group crosses swampland and encounters an older woman who summons Furiosa's clan, the Vuvalini. The Vuvalini confirm that the marshes they passed were the Green Place but it has been devastated and is no longer viable.
Recognizing that the Citadel—Joe's fortress—holds the only real chance for a sustainable life because it contains water and greenery Joe hoards, Furiosa and Max (haunted by guilt over losing a wife and child) decide to turn back and take the Citadel from Joe. They plan to lure Joe's army into the canyon trap set by the bikers. On the return run they are attacked; Furiosa is seriously wounded. On the canyon approach Joe positions his vehicle to block the War Rig. Rictus and his men battle Max while Joe pursues Furiosa. Toast distracts Joe and manages to remove his breathing apparatus; Furiosa rips the mask off, killing Immortan Joe. Nux, seeking redemption, sacrifices himself by ramming his vehicle into the enemy and wrecking the pursuing cars, killing Rictus and blocking Joe's forces, which allows Furiosa, Max, the wives, and the Vuvalini to escape driving Joe's armored car.
Furiosa is gravely injured and Max gives blood to transfuse into her so she survives. They return to the Citadel; the starving populace cheers when Joe's corpse is revealed and his regime collapses. Furiosa, the five wives (those still alive), and the Vuvalini ride onto the Citadel's platforms and are accepted by the people and even some War Boys. Max, having helped secure their future, quietly slips away into the crowd after sharing a final look with Furiosa.
original_full_tokens.setting: Post-apocalyptic desert wasteland (Citadel, War Rig, salt flats)

major_characters.name: Max Rockatansky
major_characters.description: Loner survivor captured by Immortan Joe; designated a blood donor.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Survive and find a measure of peace after losing his family; he aids Furiosa when guilt and necessity drive him to act.

major_characters.name: Imperator Furiosa
major_characters.description: One-armed lieutenant of Immortan Joe who steals the War Rig and frees Joe's wives.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Deliver Joe's wives to the Green Place (her childhood homeland) to secure their freedom and redemption from her past.

major_characters.name: Immortan Joe
major_characters.description: Tyrannical warlord who controls water at the Citadel and commands the War Boys.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Maintain power and control over resources and breeding stock to perpetuate his rule.

major_characters.name: Nux
major_characters.description: A zealous young War Boy who initially serves Joe and later seeks redemption.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Earn glory and acceptance from Immortan Joe and the War Boys; after compassion from Capable he seeks redemption by helping Furiosa's group.

major_characters.name: Capable
major_characters.description: One of Immortan Joe's five wives who becomes the emotional center for Nux and the group.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Survive and secure freedom for herself and the other wives; protect the group and inspire Nux's change.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: 1936: Indiana Jones steals a golden idol from a South American temple but triggers traps; a rolling boulder crushes his companion and the Hovitos demand the idol be returned to their leader, Dr. René Belloq, who has allied with them. Indy escapes by seaplane piloted by Jock. Back in the U.S., Museum curator Marcus Brody briefs Indy on a German radio intercept: Nazis have located Tanis and seek the Ark of the Covenant, said to grant invincibility. Army intelligence wants Indy because his former teacher, Abner Ravenwood, owned the headpiece of the Staff of Ra that locates the Ark on a Sun map. Indy travels to Nepal and finds Marion Ravenwood, Abner’s daughter, who possesses the headpiece and blames Indy for her father's death. Marion reluctantly joins for money. Nazi agent Arnold Toht and German operatives attack the bar; a fight and fire damage the headpiece. Toht sears his hand on the red-hot piece; Indy retrieves it. The headpiece shows the Staff of Ra’s correct height when paired with a staff, but a shaman in Nepal reveals the Nazis have a wrong-length staff and are digging in the wrong place. Indy and Marion go to Egypt. In Cairo, Belloq works with Wehrmacht Colonel Dietrich and Nazi archaeologists to excavate at Tanis. Indy meets Sallah, his Egyptian ally. Marion is captured once, apparently killed in a truck explosion, but later revealed alive; she is repeatedly abused and used as leverage by the Nazis. Indy sneaks into the map room beneath an excavation and, using the correctly sized staff with the headpiece at the proper sun angle, locates the Well of Souls and the Ark’s true burial site. Indy digs, finds a chamber full of snakes, and with Sallah recovers the Ark chest. Belloq seduces Marion to keep her captive; she is later thrown into the Well of Souls chamber and rescued by Indy. The Ark is seized by Nazis and packed onto a truck; Indy intercepts and retakes it, then ships it aboard a U.S. transport. A Nazi U-boat captures the transport and transfers the Ark to a mountain island where an SS ceremony is planned. Indy and Marion stow aboard the Nazi convoy, but are captured; the Ark is opened during a ritual while Indy and Marion are tied to poles. Belloq insists the Ark will reveal God’s power; when the Ark is opened, supernatural entities emerge and vaporize the Nazi officers and soldiers—Belloq is killed as the Ark’s spirits tear him apart. Indy and Marion, who kept their eyes closed, survive. Weeks later, Army officers confiscate the Ark; Indy and Marcus argue with the military, and the Ark is placed in a sealed crate and stored anonymously in a vast government warehouse.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1936; South America, Nepal, Cairo/Tanis (Egypt), Atlantic/sea routes

major_characters.name: Indiana Jones
major_characters.description: American archaeologist and university professor; adventurer and relic hunter
major_characters.primary_motivations: Recover the Ark (and survive) to keep it out of Nazi hands and protect historical artifacts.

major_characters.name: Marion Ravenwood
major_characters.description: Owner of a Kathmandu bar; daughter of Abner Ravenwood and Indy’s ex-girlfriend
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect her father’s headpiece and secure money/safety while settling a personal score with Indy.

major_characters.name: Dr. René Belloq
major_characters.description: Arrogant French archaeologist who collaborates with the Nazis
major_characters.primary_motivations: Claim the Ark for prestige and power by allying with the Nazis and outmaneuvering Indy.

major_characters.name: Colonel Dietrich / Nazi leadership (including Toht)
major_characters.description: Nazi officers and agents pursuing the Ark for military advantage
major_characters.primary_motivations: Acquire the Ark to harness its supposed supernatural power for Nazi supremacy.

major_characters.name: Sallah
major_characters.description: Egyptian excavator and ally to Indy in Cairo/Tanis
major_characters.primary_motivations: Help Indy recover the Ark and oppose Nazi control over Egyptian antiquities.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In Gotham City, mob banks are being targeted after Batman (Bruce Wayne) and Lt. James Gordon begin a campaign with new District Attorney Harvey Dent to dismantle organized crime. A gang robs a mob bank; only one survivor — a chaotic, scarred criminal calling himself the Joker — escapes with the money. The Joker (who later kills mob boss Gambol) announces he will destroy Gotham’s order and repeatedly taunts Batman, Dent, and Gordon. Bruce Wayne, suspecting financial ties to a Hong Kong accountant, sends Lucius Fox to investigate Lau of Lau's security investments. Batman travels to Hong Kong, captures Lau using a CIA sky-hook extraction, and brings Lau back to Gotham so Dent can use Lau’s knowledge to indict the mob under RICO charges. The arrests appear to be a major success — Dent gains public standing as Gotham’s “White Knight.”

The Joker retaliates: he assassinates corrupt figures (Police Commissioner Gillian B. Loeb by poisoning and Judge Surillo by car bomb) and attacks a fundraiser for Dent. Gordon is apparently killed saving Mayor Anthony Garcia; later Gordon is revealed to have faked his death to protect his family. The Joker attacks the convoy transporting Dent; a chase ends with Batman saving Dent from the Joker’s truck, and the Joker is captured and imprisoned at the Major Crimes Unit.

The Joker forces Batman and Gordon into a sadistic game: he plants simultaneous explosives targeting Rachel Dawes and Harvey Dent. He manipulates Batman into going to Dent’s location and Gordon into going to Rachel’s. Both sites explode: Rachel is killed and half of Dent’s face is horribly burned. Lau is freed by the Joker during a breakout and later killed; the Joker also burns the mob money and several mobsters, escalating chaos. Broken by Rachel’s death and manipulated by the Joker, Harvey Dent becomes Two-Face, adopting a coin-based vigilante justice and seeking revenge on those he blames.

The Joker extorts Gotham again by rigging two ferries — one with civilians, one with prisoners — each with detonators to blow up the other. Batman, with reluctant technical help from Lucius Fox, uses a citywide sonar mapping system built from every cellphone to locate the Joker’s hideout and rescues the hostages; the ferries refuse to kill one another. Batman apprehends the Joker, who gloats that Dent’s fall will destroy Gotham’s hope.

Two-Face kidnaps Gordon’s family and brings Gordon and Batman to the ruined site of Rachel’s death, intent to kill Gordon’s son for Gordon’s perceived failures. Batman, wearing body armor, tackles Two-Face off a building; Two-Face dies. To preserve Dent’s image as Gotham’s symbol of lawful hope, Batman and Gordon conceal Dent’s crimes and present Two-Face’s killings as Batman’s responsibility. Gordon smashes the Bat-Signal; Alfred burns Rachel’s letter; Lucius destroys the sonar equipment. Batman becomes Gotham’s fugitive so that Harvey Dent can remain the city’s hero, and the Joker is left alive in custody, continuing to challenge Gotham’s moral order.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day Gotham City (with brief Hong Kong sequence)

major_characters.name: Bruce Wayne / Batman
major_characters.description: Billionaire vigilante protecting Gotham; covertly investigates mob-finance links.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Stop the Joker and the mob to protect Gotham while preserving his identity and hope for a normal life.

major_characters.name: The Joker
major_characters.description: Sadistic criminal mastermind who incites chaos and personal attacks on Gotham's institutions.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Create anarchy and force Gotham's heroes to break their moral codes to prove his worldview.

major_characters.name: Harvey Dent / Two-Face
major_characters.description: Gotham's idealistic district attorney who becomes disfigured and vengeful after the Joker's attack.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Uphold justice and take down organized crime; after trauma, pursue revenge against those he blames.

major_characters.name: James Gordon
major_characters.description: Honest Gotham police lieutenant who allies with Batman and Dent and later becomes commissioner.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Reform Gotham’s police and cooperate with Batman to bring down the mob while protecting his family and the city.

major_characters.name: Rachel Dawes
major_characters.description: Gotham Assistant District Attorney and Bruce Wayne's childhood friend; Dent's girlfriend.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Prosecute corruption and support Dent; her death motivates Dent’s transformation.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In New York City, retired hitman John Wick mourns his wife Helen, who arranged a beagle puppy to help him grieve. At a gas station, Iosef Tarasov, son of Russian crime boss Viggo Tarasov, tries to buy John’s 1969 Boss 429 Mustang; John refuses. That night Iosef and two accomplices break into John’s home, beat him, steal the Mustang and kill the puppy. Aurelio, owner of a chop shop, recognizes the stolen Mustang and tells John Iosef is Viggo’s son. Viggo visits John to apologize and reveals John’s past as a feared hitman known as the “Baba Yaga,” and that John had earned his freedom by completing one final task after falling in love with Helen. John recovers a hidden cache of weapons and gold coins from a secret stash and begins hunting Iosef’s men, killing the three burglars who attacked him. Viggo places a $2 million bounty on John and hires Marcus, John’s old friend and mentor, and other killers to stop him. John checks into the Continental Hotel (run by Winston), a neutral safe haven for assassins, then goes after Iosef at the Red Circle nightclub but is forced to flee after an attack by Viggo’s henchman Kirill. A contract killer, Ms. Perkins, sneaks into John’s room to collect the bounty; Marcus warns John with a sniper shot, John incapacitates Perkins and she escapes. John finds and destroys Viggo’s cash and blackmail stash hidden in a church. Viggo confronts him; John is struck by Kirill’s car and captured, but Marcus intervenes, allowing John to kill Kirill and extract Iosef’s safe-house location. John assaults the safe house and kills Iosef. Perkins, who witnessed Marcus saving John, reveals Marcus’s role to Viggo; Viggo has Marcus arrested, tortured and executed as punishment. Viggo taunts John by phone; John ignores Marcus’s death until Viggo prepares to flee the city by helicopter. Winston has Perkins executed for breaking Continental rules. John launches a final pursuit to New York Harbor, fights Viggo and mortally wounds him. Believing he will die from his injuries, John watches a video Helen recorded telling him they need to go home. He breaks into an animal clinic, treats his wounds, rescues and adopts a pit bull puppy that was scheduled for euthanasia, and begins to walk home, alive and holding the new dog.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day New York City and underworld locations

major_characters.name: John Wick
major_characters.description: Retired hitman grieving his wife who returns to kill those who stole his last connection.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Avenge the killing of his puppy and reclaim meaning after his wife’s death by dismantling those responsible.

major_characters.name: Iosef Tarasov
major_characters.description: Impulsive son of Russian crime boss who robs John and kills the puppy.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Assert power and take what he wants out of arrogance and entitlement as Viggo’s son.

major_characters.name: Viggo Tarasov
major_characters.description: Head of the Russian mob in New York and Iosef’s father who tries to contain John.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect his son and preserve his criminal empire by stopping John’s vendetta.

major_characters.name: Marcus
major_characters.description: John’s old ally and mentor who accepts Viggo’s contract but secretly aids John.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Walk the line between loyalty and principle; protect John despite risk because of past bond.

major_characters.name: Winston
major_characters.description: Manager/owner of the Continental Hotel, neutral ground for assassins and John’s acquaintance.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Maintain the Continental’s rules and assist John within those constraints to preserve order.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In 1942 New York, frail Brooklyn native Steve Rogers repeatedly fails military physicals but is determined to serve. Dr. Abraham Erskine notices Steve's courage and recruits him for Project Rebirth, a U.S. government super-soldier program overseen by Colonel Chester Phillips. Erskine explains he fled Nazi research after an earlier experiment with Johann Schmidt (Red Skull) produced monstrous results. Erskine administers the super-soldier serum and Vita-Ray procedure; Steve's body is transformed into peak human condition. Immediately after, a Hydra spy assassinates Erskine and destroys the remaining serum; Steve chases the assassin, who kills himself, and is left the sole successful super-soldier. The War Department, led by Senator and military brass, repackages Rogers as a morale-boosting publicity figure called "Captain America," sending him on a staged bond tour with comic stage routines, much to Steve's frustration.

When Steve's friend James "Bucky" Barnes and the 107th Infantry are captured by Hydra in Europe, Steve demands to fight. Howard Stark covertly airdrops him behind enemy lines near a Hydra weapons factory. Steve infiltrates the plant, confronts Johann Schmidt (now disfigured as the Red Skull) and Arnim Zola, destroys the factory and recovers a map of Hydra bases. He rescues Bucky and about 500 POWs, proving his combat value. Chester offers formal support, but Steve insists on operating with the same team of rescued soldiers. Howard gives Steve a rounded vibranium shield for protection.

Steve leads strikes against Hydra's secret bases across Europe and the Swiss Alps, capturing scientist Arnim Zola, though Bucky dies during one raid. Zola reveals Hydra's last stronghold. Steve infiltrates the mountaintop complex and is captured and brought before Schmidt, who has harnessed the Tesseract (a mysterious cosmic energy cube) to power experimental weapons and a prototype bomber loaded with concentrated explosives labeled with U.S. city names. During an ensuing assault by Steve's team, Schmidt escapes in his jet-powered plane with the Tesseract powering its systems. Steve boards the plane, smashes the Tesseract's control console as Schmidt seizes the cube; the cube opens a space-like portal that disintegrates Schmidt. Realizing the plane will crash into populated areas, Steve pilots it away from civilians and crashes into the Arctic sea. He is reported missing and presumed dead.

Decades later, S.H.I.E.L.D. recovers Steve's body and the crashed plane; Steve is found alive but frozen. Nick Fury and S.H.I.E.L.D. revive him and gradually reintroduce him to the modern world via a staged hospital set and simulated period environments. Confused and out of time, Steve is given a choice: work for S.H.I.E.L.D. to face new global threats. In a post-credits scene, Nick Fury briefs Steve about assembling other powered individuals, showing images of Tony Stark, Thor, Bruce Banner, and others—setting up the Avengers initiative.
original_full_tokens.setting: World War II Europe and 1940s New York; modern-day (post-revival)

major_characters.name: Steve Rogers
major_characters.description: A frail Brooklyn recruit transformed into a super-soldier (Captain America).
major_characters.primary_motivations: To fight Axis forces and save his captured comrades because he believes duty and protecting others matter more than fame.

major_characters.name: Johann Schmidt / Red Skull
major_characters.description: Former Nazi scientist who leads Hydra and seeks the Tesseract's power.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To use the Tesseract and advanced weapons to gain power and reshape the world free of Nazi control.

major_characters.name: Dr. Abraham Erskine
major_characters.description: Creator of the super-soldier serum who recruits and transforms Steve.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To create a moral, human super-soldier to oppose dangerous occult and scientific experiments like Schmidt's.

major_characters.name: Peggy Carter
major_characters.description: British agent and SSR officer who assists Project Rebirth and Steve in the field.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To assist the war effort and support Steve because she respects his character and conviction.

major_characters.name: Howard Stark
major_characters.description: Inventor and U.S. defense contractor who supplies tech and covertly deploys Steve.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To stop Hydra's weapons program by assisting Steve with technology and logistical support.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: On Earth-65, Gwen Stacy (Spider-Woman) hides from a police force that suspects her of murder. After an Italian-renaissance Vulture appears through a portal, Spider-Man 2099 (Miguel O'Hara) and Jess Drew arrive and help Gwen. Miguel recruits Gwen into the Spider-Society, an interdimensional organization that polices the multiverse. On Earth-1610 (Brooklyn), Miles Morales is living with his parents Jeff and Rio while keeping his Spider-Man identity secret. The Spot (Jonathan Ohnn), an Alchemax scientist mutated by a collider, resurfaces and blames Spider-Man for his condition; he demonstrates the ability to open portals between universes and reveals that a spider from Earth-42 bit Miles a year earlier during an Alchemax experiment. Gwen returns to Earth-1610 to track the Spot and reconnects with Miles. She explains the Spider-Society but says there are no openings. When the Spot steals a collider on another world (Earth-50101) and grows stronger, Miles secretly follows Gwen through a portal. They team with Pavitr Prabhakar (Spider-Man India) and Hobie Brown (Spider-Punk) to confront the Spot. The Spot uses collider power to show Miles visions of future deaths — including Police Inspector Singh and Miles's father Jeff — then flees; the collider collapses and the world's canon event begins to break down. Members of the Spider-Society arrive to contain the damage and take Miles, Gwen, and Hobie to their headquarters on Earth-928, a massive complex where hundreds of spider-people live. Miguel explains the Society's doctrine: each universe contains fixed "canon events" (such as the death of a close figure) that must occur; deviations can destabilize and collapse entire universes and, by extension, the multiverse. Miguel reveals he witnessed a universe collapse and says Miles is an anomaly: the spider that bit Miles came from Earth-42, so Miles's existence altered multiple worlds. Miguel says saving Miles's father Jeff from a scheduled promotion-and-death would violate canon and possibly trigger collapse. Miguel imprisons Miles to stop him from interfering. Hobie quits the Society and helps Miles escape; Miguel orders the Society to capture Miles and ejects Gwen for breaking rules. Miguel confronts Miles and asserts that, because of Miles's existence, Earth-1610's Peter Parker would have survived and prevented the Spot. Miles overpowers Miguel and flees to the Society's go-home device, which identifies a target dimension by the spider DNA and sends anomalies back to their spider's home world. Miles attempts to return home but the machine sends him to Earth-42, the origin of the spider that bit him. On Earth-42 Miles finds his uncle Aaron Davis alive (Mahershala Ali) and his father dead; crime is rampant because there is no Spider-Man. He is captured by Aaron and also encounters Earth-42's version of Miles, who is the Prowler. Meanwhile Miguel forcibly returns Gwen to Earth-65; she reunites with her father George Stacy, who resigns as police captain and reconciles with her. At Society headquarters Hobie and other allies (including Peter B. Parker, Mayday Parker, Pavitr, Peni Parker, Spider-Ham, Spider-Noir and Margo Kess) assemble to find Miles. The Spot escalates his multiversal attack by hunting colliders across universes to increase his power. Gwen promises Miles's parents she'll find him. The film ends with Miles trapped on Earth-42 amid a world without its Spider-Man while the Spot continues attacking collider sites across the multiverse, setting up a larger conflict between Miles's impulse to save his father and the Spider-Society's mandate to preserve canon.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day multiverse (Earth-65, Earth-1610, Earth-928, Earth-42, others)

major_characters.name: Miles Morales
major_characters.description: Brooklyn teenager who is Spider-Man after being bitten by a spider from another dimension
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect his family and do what a hero should do, driven by guilt and responsibility for others.

major_characters.name: Gwen Stacy
major_characters.description: Spider-Woman from Earth-65 and member of the Spider-Society
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect the multiverse while reconciling her duty to the Society with her loyalty to Miles.

major_characters.name: Miguel O'Hara / Spider-Man 2099
major_characters.description: Founder and leader of the Spider-Society who enforces multiversal canon
major_characters.primary_motivations: Preserve multiversal stability by enforcing 'canon events' even if it means overriding individual lives.

major_characters.name: The Spot (Jonathan Ohnn)
major_characters.description: Alchemax scientist mutated into a portal-powered villain seeking revenge and power
major_characters.primary_motivations: Gain power across universes and punish Spider-Man for ruining his life.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In 2018 Clint Barton (Hawkeye) kills criminals after his family is snapped away by Thanos; Tony Stark and Nebula drift in space after the snap and are rescued by Carol Danvers (Captain Marvel) and returned to Earth. The surviving Avengers — Tony Stark, Steve Rogers, Natasha Romanoff, Bruce Banner, James Rhodes and Rocket — locate Thanos on the Garden planet, ambush him and learn he destroyed the Infinity Stones; Thor beheads Thanos. Five years later the world still mourns. Scott Lang (Ant-Man) returns from the Quantum Realm and proposes using quantum time travel to retrieve past Infinity Stones. The team (Stark, Rogers, Banner, Lang, Romanoff, Barton, Rocket, Nebula, Thor, Rhodes, Rocket) assemble, Stark builds a quantum GPS and Pym-particle supply is limited. They split into teams to steal stones from alternate pasts: 2012 New York (Space, Mind, Time), 2013 Asgard (Reality/aether), 2014 Morag (Power) and Vormir (Soul). In 2012 New York Banner gains the Time Stone from the Ancient One after convincing her Strange's choice was necessary; Stark and Lang lose the Tesseract to Loki. Rogers obtains the Scepter after a fight with his 2012 self. Stark and Rogers go to 1970 New Jersey to retrieve the Tesseract and extra Pym particles; Stark meets his father Howard and gains closure. On Asgard 2013, Thor retrieves the Reality Stone (Aether) with Rocket and proves himself again by calling Mjolnir. On Morag, Nebula and Rhodes collect the Power Stone but Nebula's past self is left behind and past Thanos uses the networked connection to learn the Avengers' plan, then infiltrates the future. On Vormir, Red Skull tells Natasha and Clint that the Soul Stone requires the sacrifice of a loved one; Natasha sacrifices herself so Clint can obtain the Soul Stone. The Avengers return to 2023, construct a gauntlet and Bruce Banner (as Smart Hulk) uses it to snap everyone back, severely injuring his arm. Past Nebula, captured by past Thanos in 2014, lets 2014 Thanos know of the Avengers' plan and is later killed by her future self on arrival. Thanos from 2014 brings his warship to 2023 and attacks the Avengers compound. A massive battle ensues: portals open with reinforcements (Doctor Strange, Wong, sorcerers, Wakandans, Asgardians, Ravagers) and the resurrected allies arrive. Thanos' forces overwhelm the field; the gauntlet changes hands repeatedly. Wanda Maximoff nearly kills Thanos trying to avenge Vision. Carol Danvers destroys Sanctuary II; Thanos gains the gauntlet and snaps, but Tony Stark had transferred the stones to his armor via nanotech. Tony declares "I am Iron Man" and snaps, erasing Thanos and his army at the cost of his own life. Stark dies surrounded by Pepper Potts, Rhodey and Peter Parker. The vanished return worldwide. At Tony's funeral the heroes mourn. Thor appoints Valkyrie ruler of New Asgard and joins the Guardians. Steve Rogers returns the stones and Mjolnir to their timelines but remains in the past to live a life with Peggy Carter; an elderly Steve appears, gives Sam Wilson his shield and passes the mantle of Captain America to him.
original_full_tokens.setting: Contemporary (2018–2023), multiple global and cosmic locations

major_characters.name: Tony Stark / Iron Man
major_characters.description: Genius billionaire inventor and Avenger who builds the time GPS and new gauntlet technology.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Undo the snap to restore loved ones while protecting his daughter and making amends for past failures.

major_characters.name: Steve Rogers / Captain America
major_characters.description: Former leader of the Avengers who reunites the team and leads the time-heist missions.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Right past wrongs by restoring the vanished and reconcile with Tony and his own past.

major_characters.name: Natasha Romanoff / Black Widow
major_characters.description: Avengers operative who joins the time-heist and sacrifices herself on Vormir.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Secure the Soul Stone for the mission, accepting personal sacrifice to save the universe.

major_characters.name: Thanos
major_characters.description: Mad Titan who snapped away half of all life and later invades 2023 after learning the Avengers' plan.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Recreate the universe on his terms and prevent the Avengers from undoing his work.

major_characters.name: Clint Barton / Hawkeye
