original_full_tokens.plot_summary: An unnamed narrator (Edward Norton), an insomniac cost–benefit analyst for an automobile company, cannot sleep and follows his doctor's advice to attend late-night support groups for terminally ill patients to provoke emotional catharsis. At a testicular cancer support group he bonds with Robert "Bob" Paulson and sleeps; he then cycles through other groups, faking illnesses to get the same release. His routine collapses when he spots another impostor, Marla Singer, whose presence prevents him from crying and restores his insomnia. On a business flight home he meets Tyler Durden (Brad Pitt), a charismatic soap salesman. The narrator returns to find his condominium destroyed by an explosion; with nowhere else to go he calls Tyler and, after an awkward meeting in a bar, moves into Tyler's decaying house on the condition that he hit Tyler. The pair fist-fight for release; their parking-lot brawl draws a crowd and they formalize a men-only bare-knuckle fight club in the bar basement with strict rules (never talk about Fight Club, only two to a fight, etc.). The narrator uses Fight Club to regain a feeling of control; Tyler exploits it to teach men to embrace pain and reject consumer culture. Tyler also runs small, subversive businesses—making soap from human fat, splicing porn into films at a theater, urinating in soup at a luxury hotel—and seduces Marla, who overdoses and is rescued by Tyler, beginning an affair. Under Tyler's leadership Fight Club evolves into Project Mayhem, a hierarchical, paramilitary anti-capitalist organization that recruits members from fight clubs nationwide to perform acts of vandalism and escalating sabotage. The narrator is increasingly sidelined as Tyler orchestrates more extreme missions (the mistreatment and eventual death of a Project Mayhem member, internal discipline, and urban terrorism). After Tyler disappears for periods, the narrator tries to shut down the project; traveling the country he discovers fight clubs and Project Mayhem cells in many cities and hears members call him "Tyler Durden." A visit to Marla and a series of clues lead to the revelation that Tyler is the narrator's alternate personality—Tyler controls the narrator's body when the narrator is dissociated or asleep, embodying traits the narrator lacks. Confronting this realization, the narrator attempts to stop Tyler's plan: Project Mayhem has wired explosives to the support columns of several major credit-card company high-rises. With the police compromised by Project Mayhem membership, the narrator breaks into one target building and tries to find a way to abort the demolition. He confronts Tyler and, after a struggle in which he perceives Tyler as separate, the narrator puts a gun in his mouth and fires, shooting through his cheek; the wound collapses Tyler's visualized presence. Immobilized but lucid, the narrator frees himself, finds Marla—who has been kidnapped and brought to him—and holds her as Project Mayhem detonates the explosives. From a high window they watch the simultaneous collapse of the targeted financial buildings and the city block as the film ends.
original_full_tokens.setting: Late 1990s–early 2000s, unnamed U.S. cities and corporate urban center

major_characters.name: The Narrator
major_characters.description: Unnamed insomniac automobile company cost–benefit analyst who fakes illnesses to attend support groups.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To escape crippling insomnia and emotional numbness by finding release and meaning, ultimately to stop the destructive movement he helped create.

major_characters.name: Tyler Durden
major_characters.description: Charismatic, anarchic soap salesman who organizes Fight Club and transforms it into Project Mayhem.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To dismantle consumer society and remake men's identities through violence and chaos, manifesting the narrator's suppressed urges.

major_characters.name: Marla Singer
major_characters.description: Cynical woman who also fakes attendance at support groups and becomes romantically involved with Tyler.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To survive and provoke emotional connection amid self-destructive behavior; her relationship with Tyler and the narrator exposes the narrator's fractured identity.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In 1996 aboard the research vessel Akademik Mstislav Keldysh, treasure hunter Brock Lovett and his team search the wreck of RMS Titanic for the Heart of the Ocean. They recover Caledon "Cal" Hockley's safe but find only a nude sketch dated April 14, 1912. A 101-year-old woman, Rose Dawson Calvert, contacts Lovett and identifies herself as the woman in the sketch. She visits with her granddaughter Lizzy and recounts her memories. In 1912, 17-year-old Rose DeWitt Bukater boards Titanic in Southampton with her mother Ruth DeWitt Bukater and fiancé Cal Hockley; Ruth presses the match to resolve the family's financial woes. Jack Dawson and his friend Fabrizio De Rossi win third-class tickets in a poker game and board at the last minute. Distressed by her engagement and feeling trapped, Rose climbs the stern to commit suicide but is saved by Jack. Cal invites Jack to dine with first-class passengers; Jack endures class snubs but later takes Rose to the third-class party where they dance. Rose and Jack grow closer despite Cal and Ruth's objections. Cal gifts Rose the Heart of the Ocean. Rose asks Jack to draw her nude wearing the necklace in her stateroom; she gives the drawing to Jack for safekeeping. That night, Titanic strikes an iceberg. Ship designer Thomas Andrews and officers assess five flooded compartments and conclude the ship will sink within hours. The crew launches lifeboats, many launched under capacity and with preferential treatment for first-class passengers; third-class passengers are delayed and some locked below. Cal finds Jack's drawing and, to frame Jack, slips the Heart into Jack's coat and has Jack arrested and handcuffed. Rose frees Jack as the ship fills. Cal persuades Rose to board a lifeboat but watches as she jumps back to the ship to stay with Jack. Cal fires a pistol in pursuit, then uses deception to secure a lifeboat. Titanic's stern rises, the ship breaks in two, and many passengers plunge into the freezing Atlantic. Jack and Rose cling to wreckage; Jack helps Rose onto a floating debris panel that cannot support both of them. Fifth Officer Harold Lowe rescues Rose; Jack dies of hypothermia in the water. Survivors are picked up by RMS Carpathia and taken to New York. Rose gives her name as Rose Dawson to hide from Cal. Lovett's simulated reconstruction and Rose's account confirm the sinking's timeline and Cal's attempt to save his reputation by claiming the diamond stolen. Back in 1996, Rose finishes her story aboard Lovett's vessel, goes alone to the stern at night, and drops the Heart of the Ocean into the sea above Titanic's wreck. The film closes with Rose's life montage showing she lived freely, followed by a vision/reunion of young Rose and Jack at Titanic's grand staircase among the dead.
original_full_tokens.setting: April 1912 Atlantic Ocean (RMS Titanic) and 1996 salvage ship

major_characters.name: Rose DeWitt Bukater / Rose Dawson Calvert
major_characters.description: 17-year-old first-class passenger who becomes a Titanic survivor and later a centenarian recounting her past.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Escape a controlling engagement and social constraints to find independence and true love (motivated by suffocation under her mother's and Cal's demands).

major_characters.name: Jack Dawson
major_characters.description: Poor American artist and third-class passenger who wins his Titanic ticket in a poker game.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Live freely as an artist and seize opportunities for connection and survival; he falls in love with Rose and fights to save her.

major_characters.name: Caledon 'Cal' Hockley
major_characters.description: Wealthy, possessive fiancé of Rose who seeks to secure status and control through marriage.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Maintain social standing and possession of Rose (and the Heart of the Ocean) to protect wealth and reputation.

major_characters.name: Brock Lovett
major_characters.description: Modern-day treasure hunter leading the 1996 Titanic wreck expedition.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Find the Heart of the Ocean to satisfy investors and make a major salvage discovery.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In 1981 Savannah, Georgia, Forrest Gump sits on a bus stop bench and begins recounting his life. He is born and raised in Greenbow, Alabama by his mother (Mrs. Gump), who runs a boarding house and teaches him self-respect. As a child Forrest (IQ 75) wears leg braces; on the school bus he meets Jenny Curran, his lifelong friend and love. Bullies chase Forrest; Jenny tells him to run, his braces break and he discovers he can run extremely fast. Forrest earns a football scholarship to the University of Alabama, plays under Coach Bear Bryant, returns a book to Vivian Malone during Governor George Wallace’s “schoolhouse door” incident, and meets President John F. Kennedy at the White House.

After college (1967) Forrest enlists in the U.S. Army. He befriends Private Benjamin “Bubba” Blue, who teaches him about shrimping, and Lieutenant Dan Taylor, the platoon leader descended from soldiers who died in U.S. wars. In Vietnam Forrest writes to Jenny but she doesn’t reply; during an ambush Bubba is killed and Forrest carries several wounded soldiers, including Lt. Dan, to safety. Forrest is shot in the buttocks, receives the Medal of Honor from President Lyndon B. Johnson, and is discharged. Lt. Dan loses his legs and initially resents Forrest for saving him.

While recovering at a VA hospital Forrest learns ping-pong and becomes a national ping-pong celebrity, travels to China with the U.S. team (ping-pong diplomacy), appears on television with John Lennon, and meets President Richard Nixon. Forrest inadvertently witnesses suspicious activity at the Watergate complex when staying there with the ping-pong team. Returning to Greenbow, Forrest uses endorsement money to buy a shrimp boat in Bayou La Batre to honor Bubba’s dream. He starts the Bubba Gump Shrimp Company; after Hurricane Carmen their fleet prospers. Lt. Dan, who joined Forrest as first mate, recuperates emotionally and financially when he invests Forrest’s profits in Apple stock; Forrest gives half the earnings to Bubba’s family.

Forrest’s mother dies of cancer in 1974. He periodically reunites with Jenny, who pursues a self-destructive life of drugs and abuse. In 1972 Jenny briefly rekindles with Forrest at an anti-war rally; she later leaves. In 1976 Jenny returns; Forrest proposes, they sleep together, and she tells him she loves him but leaves the next morning. Devastated, Forrest runs across America for three years, becoming a cultural phenomenon, then stops and returns to Greenbow.

In 1981 Forrest receives a letter from Jenny asking him to visit. He reunites with her; she has a young son named Forrest Jr., whom she reveals is Forrest’s child. Jenny is ill (implied hepatitis C); they marry and move back to Greenbow. Jenny dies about a year later. The film ends with Forrest seeing his son off on his first day of school and waiting at the bus stop to finish his story.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1950s–1980s United States (Greenbow, Alabama; Vietnam; Bayou La Batre; Savannah)

major_characters.name: Forrest Gump
major_characters.description: Slow‑witted but kind man from Greenbow, Alabama whose life intersects major historical events.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To love and be with Jenny and to honor promises (shaped by his mother's lessons).

major_characters.description: Forrest’s childhood friend and troubled love interest who drifts through radical/abusive lifestyles.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To escape her abusive past and find freedom, which leads her into self‑destructive choices and away from stability.

major_characters.description: Forrest’s platoon leader in Vietnam who loses his legs and struggles with destiny and bitterness.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To fulfill his family’s military legacy and reclaim purpose after losing his legs; ultimately seeks redemption and control over his fate.

major_characters.name: Benjamin “Bubba” Blue
major_characters.description: Forrest’s friend and fellow soldier who knows the shrimping business.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To start a shrimping business and secure a stable future for his family.

major_characters.name: Mrs. Gump
major_characters.description: Forrest’s mother who raises him in Greenbow and runs a boarding house.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To protect Forrest and ensure he is treated with dignity and belief in himself.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In 1990s Seoul, Na-young (later Nora Moon) and Hae-sung are childhood classmates who walk home together and tell each other they will marry someday. Na-young’s family prepares to emigrate to Canada; on the night before they leave she asks Hae-sung to wait for her, and he promises. At age twelve she boards the plane and they lose daily contact. Years later Na-young, now using the name Nora, lives in New York, has become a successful playwright, and is married to Arthur, an American. Hae-sung stays in Korea, studies engineering, completes mandatory military service, and works in Seoul; he keeps sporadic contact with Nora by video chat and email through their late teens and twenties. Their conversations are intimate and frequently nostalgic but noncommittal.

When Hae-sung travels to New York in his early thirties, Nora arranges to meet him with Arthur. The three meet in a bar and then spend time together in Nora and Arthur’s apartment. Hae-sung and Nora reconnect quickly: they revisit shared memories, walk through neighborhoods, and spend long one-on-one conversations about choices and what might have been. Hae-sung reveals he left a stable life to travel and has unresolved feelings; Nora is candid that she loves Arthur but is unsettled by how easily she slips into intimacy with Hae-sung. Arthur notices the closeness and feels like an outsider; he alternates between warmth and guarded jealousy. Over several days Nora and Hae-sung test boundaries—kissing twice and sharing a night together where they talk through their adolescent vows and current lives, but neither pursues a full affair nor immediately leaves Arthur.

Back in Seoul after the visit, Hae-sung decides to move to New York to be near Nora; he takes the risk of emigrating and finds work. Nora faces mounting guilt and confusion; she tells Arthur the truth about her emotional infidelity and her physical intimacy with Hae-sung. Arthur reacts with pain but also with pragmatic attempts to preserve their marriage. Nora and Arthur attempt couples therapy, and Nora informs Hae-sung she cannot break her marriage lightly; she needs to know if his decision to move is permanent. Hae-sung, committed to following Nora, rents an apartment and plans to build a life close by.

Months later, Nora and Hae-sung meet one last time in New York to confront whether to pursue a life together. Nora ultimately chooses to stay with Arthur for the stability and history of their marriage; she declines to reopen her life to the uncertainty of leaving. Hae-sung, disappointed but resolute, accepts her decision and begins to rebuild his own life independently. The film closes with each character moving forward separately: Nora remaining with Arthur while carrying the memory of what might have been, and Hae-sung starting anew in New York, honoring the past connection but relinquishing the expectation of a romantic future together.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1990s–2000s Seoul and modern-day New York City

major_characters.name: Nora Moon (Na-young)
major_characters.description: South Korean girl who emigrates, becomes a New York playwright and marries Arthur.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To reconcile her childhood bond with Hae-sung with her commitment to Arthur and the life she built abroad.

major_characters.name: Hae-sung
major_characters.description: Na-young’s childhood best friend from Seoul who later works in Korea and then moves to New York.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To reunite with Nora and pursue the relationship he and his twelve-year-old self believed in.

major_characters.name: Arthur
major_characters.description: Nora’s American husband, a New Yorker who tries to maintain his marriage.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To preserve his marriage to Nora and confront the threat posed by Hae-sung to their life together.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In September 1939, Wladyslaw Szpilman, a celebrated Polish-Jewish radio pianist in Warsaw, plays live on the radio when the station is bombed during the German invasion. He returns home to his comfortable family apartment to find Warsaw increasingly under Nazi control: Jews must wear armbands, lose jobs, and face public humiliation. The Szpilman family sell belongings, including his piano, and by November 1940 are forced into the overcrowded Warsaw Ghetto. Wladyslaw finds work performing in a cafe inside the ghetto. On 16 August 1942 the family is rounded up for deportation to the Treblinka extermination camp; at the Umschlagplatz a friend in the Jewish Ghetto Police separates Szpilman from the line, letting him escape while the rest of his family is sent away and murdered. Szpilman becomes a forced laborer dismantling parts of the ghetto and helps smuggle weapons for the planned uprising, but soon decides to escape into the non-Jewish city.

With help from friends — Andrzej Bogucki and his wife Janina, caretaker Gebczynski, and his friend Dorota — Szpilman goes into hiding in a series of empty apartments and rooms in Warsaw. He lives in near-silence on smuggled food, observes the April 1943 Warsaw Ghetto Uprising from a hiding place, and narrowly avoids capture several times. When his first hiding apartment must be abandoned because resistance weapons are discovered, Gebczynski gives Szpilman an emergency address and leaves. Szpilman later breaks some dishes, alerting neighbors, and flees to Dorota, who arranges another hiding place in the German quarter with a man named Szalas. Szalas proves unreliable and pockets money meant for Szpilman, leaving him ill and near starvation; Dorota and her husband obtain a doctor to treat his jaundice.

In August 1944 the Polish Home Army launches the Warsaw Uprising; the Germans respond by razing the city and evacuating or killing civilians. Szpilman’s building is destroyed by shells; he flees through fighting and hides in an abandoned hospital, then jumps into the deserted ghetto and wanders among the ruins as the city is systematically demolished. Alone and starving, he scavenges for food and shelter. In late 1944 he breaks into an abandoned house and is discovered by Wehrmacht officer Wilm Hosenfeld, who recognizes Szpilman and, upon hearing him play Chopin on a ruined grand piano, spares him. Hosenfeld secretly brings Szpilman food and allows him to hide in the attic; the two develop a guarded, human connection. In January 1945, with the Red Army approaching, Hosenfeld gives Szpilman his greatcoat and promises to help him after the war, then leaves. During the German retreat Szpilman is nearly killed when Polish liberating troops mistake him for a German because of the coat; they shoot and then take him into custody and save his life.

After the war Szpilman survives and returns to music. He later hears that Hosenfeld was captured by the Soviets; in 1947 Szpilman learns Hosenfeld died in Soviet captivity in 1952. The film ends with Szpilman performing publicly as a celebrated pianist and an epilogue noting he died in 2000 at age 88.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1939–1945, Nazi-occupied Warsaw, Poland

major_characters.name: Wladyslaw Szpilman
major_characters.description: Polish-Jewish radio pianist forced into the Warsaw Ghetto who survives by hiding in the ruined city.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Survive Nazi persecution and reunite with family, using music and help from friends to stay alive.

major_characters.name: Wilm Hosenfeld
major_characters.description: German Wehrmacht officer who discovers Szpilman in ruins and secretly aids him after hearing him play piano.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Maintain duty as an officer while responding to Szpilman's humanity and musical talent by protecting him.

major_characters.name: Dorota
major_characters.description: Non-Jewish friend and contact who helps hide and provide medicine and addresses for Szpilman.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect Szpilman out of friendship and moral opposition to Nazi persecution, arranging hiding places and aid.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In a modern-day nursing home, an elderly man who goes by Duke (James Garner) reads from a leather-bound notebook daily to a female patient named Allie. The notebook recounts a 1940s romance on Seabrook Island, South Carolina. In 1940, 17-year-old Allison “Allie” Hamilton (Rachel McAdams), an affluent heiress, meets poor lumber mill worker Noah Calhoun (Ryan Gosling) at a carnival. Noah pursues her; after dramatic flirting on a ferris wheel he coerces her into a date. They fall passionately in love, spending the summer together; Noah teaches Allie to swim and drive, and they plan a future in an abandoned Windsor Plantation house Noah intends to buy and restore to her specifications (white house, blue shutters, water-view room, wraparound porch). Allie’s mother Anne (Joan Allen) disapproves of Noah’s class and forbids contact. After a heated argument, Allie impulsively breaks up with Noah; her parents move the family back to Charleston and Anne intercepts Noah’s letters so Allie never receives them. Noah writes every day for a year (365 letters) and then stops when none are returned. Noah enlists with his friend Fin (Kevin Connolly) and fights in World War II; Fin dies in the Battle of the Bulge. Allie volunteers in a hospital, meets Captain Lon Hammond Jr. (James Marsden), a wealthy suitor, and becomes engaged. After the war Noah returns, restores the Windsor house exactly to Allie’s specs, and refuses to sell. Allie sees a newspaper photo of Noah’s finished house, takes a pre-wedding trip to visit Seabrook, and reunites with Noah; they consummate their love. Noah reveals he wrote to her daily and that Anne intercepted the letters. Anne admits she once loved beneath her class and, guilt-ridden, returns the hidden letters to Allie. Allie tells Lon the truth and breaks the engagement, recommitting to Noah. In the nursing home present, Duke is revealed to be Noah using the name to avoid frightening his disoriented wife; the woman is elderly Allie (Gena Rowlands) suffering from advanced dementia/Alzheimer’s. Noah has recorded or preserved their story (Allie’s own journal) and reads it to her each day to jog her memory. Near the notebook’s end, Allie briefly recognizes Noah, asks how long the memory will last, and he says perhaps five minutes; they dance to their song. Allie’s dementia relapses; she panics and is sedated. Noah has a heart attack and is hospitalized in the same facility. That night Noah sneaks into Allie’s room; Allie again briefly remembers, they reassure each other about their life and children, embrace, and fall asleep together. In the morning a nurse finds both dead in each other’s arms.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1940s Seabrook Island/Charleston, modern-day nursing home

major_characters.name: Noah Calhoun
major_characters.description: Poor lumber mill worker who restores an old house and loves Allie across decades.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Reunite with and preserve his life with Allie because he believes their love is lifelong.

major_characters.name: Allison "Allie" Hamilton
major_characters.description: Wealthy young heiress whose family separates her from Noah; later suffers dementia.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Choose between social expectations (and Lon) and her love for Noah, and later to recover memory of their life together.

major_characters.description: Allie’s domineering mother who forbids the relationship and hides Noah’s letters.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Protect her daughter’s social standing by keeping Allie from Noah, driven by class pride and fear of scandal.

major_characters.name: Lon Hammond Jr.
major_characters.description: Charming, well-bred suitor and Allie’s fiancé from old Southern money.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Marry Allie and provide social stability and approval that her family desires.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
Henry Roth, a charismatic veterinarian at Sea Life Park on Oahu, Hawaii, lives a commitment-free life specializing in walruses while saving for a year-long walrus study trip to Bristol Bay. After his sailboat Sea Serpent is disabled, Henry stops at the Hukilau Cafe and meets Lucy Whitmore, a local-looking artist. They hit it off and agree to meet the next morning, but Lucy fails to recognize him. Sue, the cafe owner, explains that Lucy suffers from anterograde amnesia from a car accident that occurred a year earlier on her father Marlin’s birthday; she wakes each morning believing it is that day and cannot form new memories. To spare Lucy daily trauma, Marlin and Lucy’s brother Doug re-enact the same Sunday routine (newspaper, shampoo bottles, a Vikings game, The Sixth Sense) every evening so she wakes to the scripted day.

Henry pursues Lucy anyway, repeatedly reintroducing himself after she forgets him each night. He learns Lucy only sings the Beach Boys’ "Wouldn't It Be Nice" on days she has met someone new, which persuades Marlin and Doug to let Henry keep seeing her. Henry’s friends (Ula, Alexa) and his life at Sea Life Park provide background friction; Henry gradually gives up parts of his playboy lifestyle. After Henry accidentally hurts Lucy’s feelings, he apologizes and continues to engineer meetings. When Lucy briefly realizes the calendar date after a parking-ticket incident, Marlin and Doug confess the daily ruse; Lucy is devastated.

Henry makes a video that explains Lucy’s accident, her condition, and documents their relationship; Marlin and Doug show it to her every morning so her days can continue without repeating the traumatic accident. Lucy spends more time with Henry and old friends and begins painting again. Henry ultimately reveals he is postponing his Bristol Bay research trip for a year to try to build a life with Lucy; overhearing this, Lucy asks him to erase their relationship for his sake. They destroy her journal entries and Henry prepares to leave. Marlin later tells Henry that Lucy moved into the brain-injury institute and teaches an art class there; Henry sails away but then recalls that Lucy only sings after meeting him, prompting him to return.

At the institute Henry discovers Lucy does not consciously remember him, but she has painted dozens of portraits of a man who is clearly Henry and says she dreams of him nightly. The film ends with Lucy watching a "Good Morning Lucy" tape that Henry made: it explains her life and concludes with footage of their wedding and a recorded instruction from Henry to put on a jacket and come have breakfast. The final scene reveals Henry, Marlin, and Lucy together on Henry’s boat in Alaska with their young daughter Nicole, showing that Henry stayed, Lucy retained emotional memory through dreams and art, and they formed a family despite her anterograde amnesia.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day Oahu, Hawaii (also Bristol Bay, Alaska briefly)

major_characters.name: Henry Roth
major_characters.description: Sea Life Park veterinarian and lifelong commitment-phobe who woos tourists
major_characters.primary_motivations: To avoid attachments until he falls for Lucy, then to win and keep her love despite her memory loss.

major_characters.name: Lucy Whitmore
major_characters.description: Local artist who has anterograde amnesia from a car accident
major_characters.primary_motivations: To live a stable, untraumatized daily life and later to reclaim agency over her relationships despite not forming new memories.

major_characters.name: Marlin Whitmore
major_characters.description: Lucy's father who stages daily routines to protect Lucy from reliving her accident
major_characters.primary_motivations: To shield Lucy from emotional harm by maintaining a controlled daily script and protect her well-being.

major_characters.name: Doug Whitmore
major_characters.description: Lucy's older brother who helps maintain Lucy's scripted days
major_characters.primary_motivations: To protect Lucy while testing Henry’s intentions and eventually support whatever helps Lucy most.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
Anna Brady, a Boston real estate stager, is frustrated that her cardiologist boyfriend Jeremy Sloane has not proposed after four years. She plans to surprise him by flying to Dublin to propose on February 29, invoking an Irish leap-day tradition. En route her plane is diverted by a storm to Wales; she charters a boat but the storm forces it to put her ashore in the seaside village of Dingle, County Kerry. At a local tavern she meets surly innkeeper Declan O'Callaghan. Declan initially refuses to drive her to Dublin but agrees when his pub is threatened with foreclosure and Anna offers €500 to cover much of his debt. They set off in Declan's beat-up car with Anna's expensive Louis Vuitton suitcase (“Louie”).

Their cross-country journey becomes a series of misadventures: cows block a rural road and Anna steps in cow dung; while attempting to clean her shoes her handbrake-less car rolls into a stream; three travelers steal Anna's luggage and Declan fights them to recover it; they miss a train after exploring castle ruins and spend a night at a bed-and-breakfast in Tipperary where they pretend to be married and are forced to kiss at dinner. Their antagonistic banter gradually softens into mutual attraction. During a hail storm they shelter in a church hosting a wedding reception; Anna drinks too much, begins to question her motives toward Jeremy, and realizes she has feelings for Declan.

They arrive in Dublin the day before/ morning of February 29. Declan reveals he was once engaged and that his ex ran off to Dublin with his mother's claddagh ring and his best friend. At the hotel where Jeremy is staying, Jeremy unexpectedly proposes to Anna in the lobby; Anna accepts but glances back to see Declan leaving. Back in Boston at their engagement party, Anna learns Jeremy proposed to satisfy a co-op board condition for their apartment purchase. Alarmed by how material and self-interested Jeremy is (he grabs electronics when Anna pulls a fire alarm to simulate fleeing), Anna realizes she does not love him. Meanwhile Declan retrieves his mother's claddagh ring from his ex in Dublin. Anna returns to Dingle, finds Declan running his tavern successfully after rallying community support to pay his debts, and tells him she broke off the engagement and wants to be with him. Declan briefly leaves; he returns at the cliff overlooking the sea with his mother's ring and proposes to Anna. She accepts. The film ends with Anna and Declan together, leaving their future and destination open.
original_full_tokens.setting: Modern-day Ireland (Dingle, County Kerry to Dublin) and Boston

major_characters.name: Anna Brady
major_characters.description: Boston real estate stager determined to control her life and get engaged.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To secure a marriage to Jeremy by proposing on leap day because she believes the tradition will force his acceptance.

major_characters.name: Declan O'Callaghan
major_characters.description: Surly Dingle tavern/inn owner struggling with debt and foreclosure.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To save his pub from foreclosure and reclaim his mother's claddagh ring, while resisting emotional attachment.

major_characters.name: Jeremy Sloane
major_characters.description: Anna's long-term boyfriend, a pragmatic cardiologist focused on career and material stability.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To secure an apartment and maintain social/material stability, causing him to propose for practical reasons rather than love.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
Anastasia “Ana” Steele, a 21-year-old English literature student at Washington State University's satellite campus near Vancouver, Washington, fills in for her sick roommate Kate Kavanagh to interview wealthy Seattle entrepreneur Christian Grey for the college paper. Christian, a 27-year-old telecommunications magnate and control-focused billionaire, takes a pronounced interest in Ana. He offers her an internship (which she declines), later visits the hardware store where she works, and arranges a photo shoot to accompany the article. After sending Ana first editions of Thomas Hardy novels (including Tess of the d'Urbervilles) and leaving a cryptic card, Christian pursues her with coffees and gifts. Ana, a romantic and inexperienced woman, is flattered and begins dating Christian. After Ana drunkenly calls him on graduation night, Christian finds her passed out in a bar and brings her to his hotel; they do not sleep together but share a charged kiss and begin a relationship.

Christian requires Ana to sign a non-disclosure agreement and presents her with a formal contract that defines their sexual arrangement: Ana would be a submissive in a BDSM relationship, with sex confined to a designated “playroom” and specific rules and limits. Ana reveals she is a virgin; Christian takes care to make her first intercourse outside the bondage practices, then introduces her gradually to bondage (starting with a silk tie). Christian showers Ana with expensive gifts (a car, electronics) and attempts to control many aspects of their interactions. He tells her about his upbringing—adopted at four, emotionally guarded—and that he had a formative abusive/submissive relationship as a teenager. He also introduces Ana to his adoptive parents (Mr. and Mrs. Grey) and sister Mia.

Ana negotiates the contract, striking out clauses she cannot accept and securing one reserved date night a week. Christian asks Ana to try his sexual preferences before deciding; she agrees and experiences the “playroom” and bondage sessions. As their intimacy deepens, Ana becomes more unsettled by Christian's emotional distance and by the clinical, punitive elements of his expectations. Christian insists on maintaining strict control and demands obedience; he is reluctant to engage in typical romantic gestures. During a later visit to Ana's mother in Georgia, Christian impulsively appears, revealing his controlling impulses. Ana increasingly researches BDSM and submissive dynamics but remains conflicted.

When Ana asks Christian to demonstrate how he would punish her for breaking rules, he lashes her backside six times with a belt while making her count—an act he frames as negotiation of limits but which traumatizes her. Disturbed by the physical punishment, Christian's coldness afterward, and the realization that he wants a relationship structured around control and pain rather than mutual emotional intimacy, Ana concludes their arrangement is unhealthy for her. She leaves Christian, ending the relationship.

The film closes on Ana's decision to walk away after recognizing that Christian's need for dominance, secrecy, and punishment makes him dangerous for any partner and that she cannot submit to a life defined by his terms.
original_full_tokens.setting: Contemporary Seattle area (Vancouver, WA; Seattle) and Georgia

major_characters.name: Anastasia "Ana" Steele
major_characters.description: 21-year-old English literature student from Vancouver, Washington; romantic and naive.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To explore a relationship with Christian while seeking emotional connection and preserving her own limits.

major_characters.name: Christian Grey
major_characters.description: 27-year-old wealthy Seattle entrepreneur and control-oriented BDSM practitioner with a troubled past.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To maintain dominance and control in intimate relationships as a way to manage his trauma and needs.

major_characters.name: Kate Kavanagh
major_characters.description: Ana's fellow student and roommate who originally was to interview Christian.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To help Ana and further her own journalism; indirectly enables Ana's meeting with Christian.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In 1934 Hercule Poirot, a famed Belgian detective, solves a theft in Jerusalem then travels toward London via the Simplon Orient Express after his friend Bouc (a director at the train company) secures him a berth. On the train Poirot is offered by businessman Samuel Ratchett (who later is identified as John Cassetti) to be his bodyguard; Poirot refuses. That night Poirot hears noises from Ratchett's compartment and sees someone in a red kimono in the corridor. An avalanche derails and stalls the train in Yugoslavia, trapping the first-class passengers. The next morning Ratchett is found stabbed to death multiple times in his locked cabin. Bouc and Poirot begin an investigation while local authorities await the train being freed. Evidence collected: Ratchett's coffee was drugged; a button from a conductor's uniform and a bloodstained handkerchief are found in Mrs. Hubbard's compartment; a torn, partially burned note links Ratchett to the 1929 kidnapping and murder of Daisy Armstrong; a red kimono and a conductor's uniform are discovered; Mrs. Hubbard (Caroline Hubbard) is stabbed but survives and later is revealed to be Linda Arden, Daisy's grandmother. Poirot interviews each passenger: Hector MacQueen (Ratchett's secretary), Edward Henry Masterman (Ratchett's valet), Colonel John Arbuthnot, Mary Debenham (governess), Count and Countess Andrenyi (the Countess later identified as Helena Goldenberg), Princess Natalia Dragomiroff, Greta Ohlsson (nurse), Pilar Estravados (nurse), Antonio Foscarelli (chauffeur), Pierre Michel (conductor), Cyrus Hardman (private detective), and Fräulein Hildegarde Schmidt (maid). Poirot uncovers that Ratchett is actually John Cassetti, the kidnapper of Daisy Armstrong whose crime destroyed the Armstrong family: Sonia Armstrong died after premature labor, Colonel Armstrong later killed himself, the Armstrong maid Susanne was arrested and hanged herself in custody, and many relatives and associates were ruined. Poirot finds that many passengers have direct connections to the Armstrongs or to Susanne: Masterman had served Colonel Armstrong; MacQueen's father was the district attorney on the case; Princess Dragomiroff and Linda Arden are family friends/relatives; the Countess is Sonia's sister; Michel is Susanne's grieving father; Hardman loved Susanne; Mary Debenham was Daisy's governess; Foscarelli was the family chauffeur; Greta and Pilar cared for Daisy. Poirot also discovers attempts to fabricate evidence (burned ledgers, tampered passports) and that several items were planted to suggest an external killer. When Colonel Arbuthnot later storms the compartment and shoots at Poirot, he reveals he never intended to kill Poirot but to force a confession. Poirot assembles the passengers and offers two solutions to the Yugoslavian police: (1) a lone assassin boarded the train disguised as a conductor and escaped; or (2) the more complex truth—that all thirteen implicated passengers and the conductor participated in a planned collective murder of Cassetti, each taking turns stabbing him to avenge Daisy Armstrong and the harm done to her family. Mrs. Hubbard admits she organized the meeting and planned the murder; the group confirms their motives and actions. Poirot exposes that the gun presented to him was unloaded to test reactions and that Arbuthnot’s staged shooting was meant to demonstrate the conspirators' resolve. Bouc and Poirot agree on a course of action: present the lone killer story to the authorities so the passengers can go free. Poirot, who prides himself on truth and balance, is forced to live with this moral compromise because he judges Cassetti deserved death. The train resumes; the suspects disperse and are allowed to leave. As Poirot disembarks a messenger asks him to investigate a death in Egypt (Death on the Nile), and he accepts.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1934, aboard the Simplon Orient Express (Europe, halted in Yugoslavia)

major_characters.name: Hercule Poirot
major_characters.description: Famed Belgian detective traveling on the Orient Express.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To discover who murdered Ratchett/Cassetti and reconcile truth with justice.

major_characters.name: Samuel Ratchett / John Cassetti
major_characters.description: American businessman aboard the train who is revealed to be a child kidnapper and murderer.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To protect himself from threats tied to his past crimes; hires (then is refused) bodyguard protection.

major_characters.name: Bouc
major_characters.description: Poirot's friend and Orient Express director's nephew who arranges Poirot's passage.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To help Poirot investigate and to mediate between Poirot and the other passengers.

major_characters.name: Linda Arden / Mrs. Hubbard
major_characters.description: American former actress traveling as Mrs. Hubbard; Daisy Armstrong's grandmother and organizer of the plot.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To avenge granddaughter Daisy Armstrong's kidnapping and death by gathering and organizing those wronged.

major_characters.name: Colonel John Arbuthnot
major_characters.description: Former army officer and friend of the Armstrong family.
major_characters.primary_motivations: To avenge Colonel Armstrong and Daisy Armstrong by participating in Cassetti's murder.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
Evelyn Quan Wang, a middle-aged Chinese-American immigrant who runs a laundromat in the U.S. with her husband Waymond Wang, is overwhelmed by family tensions and an IRS audit. Waymond secretly prepares divorce papers to force a conversation; their adult daughter Joy is estranged, depressed, and in a relationship with Becky, which Evelyn struggles to accept. Evelyn's demanding father (Gong Gong) arrives from China for Chinese New Year, increasing pressure. During a fraught audit meeting with IRS inspector Deirdre Beaubeirdre, an alternate-reality version of Waymond (Alpha‑Waymond) suddenly takes over Waymond's body and informs Evelyn that countless parallel universes exist and that Jobu Tupaki — a splintered, omnipotent version of Evelyn's daughter (Alpha‑Joy) — is threatening the multiverse. Alpha‑Waymond gives Evelyn “verse-jumping” technology that lets her access skills and memories of her alternate selves by performing bizarre actions.

As Evelyn verse-jumps, she discovers lives she could have lived (a kung-fu star, a chef, a wealthy and confident Evelyn) and begins manifesting those abilities in her primary body. Jobu Tupaki, who experiences every universe simultaneously and created an ‘everything bagel’ singularity capable of destroying the multiverse, sends minions and corrupted versions of people (including an alternate Deirdre and Alpha‑Gong Gong) to capture Evelyn. Alpha‑Waymond believes this Evelyn — whom other alphas call a ‘failure’ — has latent potential to stop Jobu. Alpha‑Gong Gong, convinced extreme measures are needed, instructs Evelyn to kill Joy to prevent Jobu from using her as a conduit; Evelyn refuses.

Evelyn escalates verse-jumping to match Jobu's power. Jobu kills Alpha‑Waymond in the alphaverse and confronts Evelyn in Evelyn’s universe, showing her the everything bagel and preaching that nothing matters. Evelyn briefly succumbs to nihilism: in several jumped realities she behaves cruelly (she signs the divorce papers, destroys the laundromat, hurts loved ones) and nearly enters the bagel with Jobu. In Evelyn’s home world, Waymond persistently appeals to ordinary kindness and connection; his simple plea for people to be kinder and more patient snaps Evelyn back. She rejects Jobu's annihilation, reconciles with Waymond, accepts Joy and Becky, and tells Gong Gong the truth about Joy’s relationship. Evelyn uses verse-jumping not to copy Jobu's nihilism but to bring empathy into fights — pulling compassionate memories and small, specific skills from other selves — which undoes damage across universes and neutralizes Jobu’s allies (including Alpha‑Gong Gong and the minions).

Joy, anguished, attempts to join Jobu in the bagel; Evelyn refuses to sacrifice her and instead reaches Joy with a promise that even if nothing makes sense, she chooses to be with her. Together they rescue Jobu from the bagel; Jobu, confronted with care and connection, ceases her campaign. The family repairs relationships: Evelyn, Waymond, Joy, Gong Gong, and even Deirdre return to finalize the IRS audit and refile taxes. Evelyn remains aware of other selves but grounds herself in her life, choosing patience, love, and small acts of kindness over nihilism. The film ends with the family reconciled and Evelyn present in her original universe.
original_full_tokens.setting: Contemporary U.S. (laundromat, IRS office, multiversal landscapes)

major_characters.name: Evelyn Quan Wang
major_characters.description: Middle-aged Chinese-American laundromat owner and mother.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Reconcile her failing family life and survive the multiversal crisis by accessing alternate selves to stop Jobu and protect her daughter.

major_characters.name: Waymond Wang
major_characters.description: Evelyn's gentle husband and laundromat co-owner.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Repair his marriage to Evelyn by forcing honest conversation and help her choose compassion over nihilism.

major_characters.name: Joy Wang / Jobu Tupaki
major_characters.description: Evelyn and Waymond's adult daughter; Joy becomes Jobu Tupaki in the alphaverse, a splintered omnipotent antagonist.
major_characters.primary_motivations: As Joy, seek acceptance and purpose; as Jobu, express despair and desire to end meaningless existence by creating the everything bagel.

major_characters.name: Gong Gong
major_characters.description: Evelyn's critical elderly father who visits from China.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Maintain cultural and familial authority; his judgment pushes Evelyn toward self-examination and conflict.

major_characters.name: Deirdre Beaubeirdre
major_characters.description: Hard-nosed IRS auditor overseeing Evelyn's tax audit.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Enforce tax law and close the audit, unintentionally catalyzing Evelyn's multiversal awakening during the meeting.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary:
In Toronto, 22-year-old Scott Pilgrim is the bass player for garage band Sex Bob-Omb and is dating 17-year-old high-school student Knives Chau. Scott is still reeling from being dumped by ex Natalie "Envy" Adams. One night Scott sees American delivery girl Ramona Flowers and becomes obsessed. He begins courting Ramona while still nominally dating Knives; his friends Wallace Wells, Stephen Stills, Kim Pine and his sister Stacey urge him to break up with Knives but he delays. Scott arranges deliveries so Ramona must sign for packages and asks her out; after a snowstorm they shelter in Ramona’s apartment and begin to kiss, though Ramona refuses sex on their first date. At the Battle of the Bands, Scott is attacked by Matthew Patel, who announces himself as one of Ramona’s seven "evil exes." Scott defeats Patel in a brief fight and Ramona explains that any new boyfriend must defeat all seven exes who control her love life.

Scott finally breaks up with Knives after she confesses love for him at her birthday dinner; Knives vows to win him back and begins imitating Ramona. The second evil ex, actor/skateboarder Lucas Lee, attacks Scott; Scott tricks Lucas into a fatal skateboard stunt and defeats him. The third ex, Todd Ingram—boyfriend of Scott’s ex Envy and a member of her band—uses telekinetic "vegan" powers to overpower Scott at a concert, but Scott and Sex Bob-Omb trick Todd into drinking coffee with cream, causing the Vegan Police to revoke his powers; Scott then kills Todd in battle. Scott fights Roxanne "Roxy" Richter, a former lesbian fling of Ramona’s, and Ramona intervenes to protect Scott; after Roxy’s defeat tensions rise as Scott becomes resentful of Ramona’s past and lashes out, so Ramona breaks up with him.

During a later Battle of the Bands, Sex Bob-Omb faces the twin Katayanagi brothers (exes five and six) and defeats them, earning Scott an "extra life." Scott returns home to find Sex Bob-Omb’s sponsor is Gideon Graves, who is revealed to be Ramona’s seventh and most powerful evil ex. The band signs Gideon’s record deal against Scott’s wishes; Scott leaves. Gideon invites Scott to the Chaos Theatre where Gideon formally confronts him. Scott challenges Gideon, gains a flimsy "power of love" sword which Gideon destroys, and then Knives storms the stage to fight Ramona. Scott tries to stop them and in the confusion confesses he cheated on both girls; Gideon kills Scott. In limbo, Ramona apologizes and reveals Gideon had implanted a subdermal device in her—Gideon used mind-control to keep exes in line. Scott uses his unused extra life to return to the moment before Gideon’s fatal blow.

Scott returns to the Chaos Theatre, reconciles with his friends, and fights Gideon again, this time earning the stronger "power of self-respect" sword. Scott apologizes to Ramona and Knives for his betrayals; Gideon intervenes, knocks Ramona down, and he, Scott and Knives team up to defeat Gideon. With Gideon destroyed and the mind-control broken, Ramona prepares to leave Toronto. Knives accepts that Scott is no longer hers and urges him to follow Ramona. Scott pursues Ramona, they reconcile honestly, and begin a relationship anew as Sex Bob-Omb continues playing and Scott moves forward having defeated the seven evil exes.
original_full_tokens.setting: Contemporary Toronto, primarily clubs and apartments

major_characters.name: Scott Pilgrim
major_characters.description: 22-year-old unemployed bass guitarist of Sex Bob-Omb; protagonist.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Win Ramona Flowers' love by defeating her seven evil exes and find personal growth after past heartbreak.

major_characters.name: Ramona Flowers
major_characters.description: American delivery girl with a mysterious past and seven powerful exes.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Leave her past behind and be free to have a peaceful life in Toronto.

major_characters.name: Knives Chau
major_characters.description: 17-year-old high-school girlfriend of Scott who becomes determined to win him back.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Hold onto her relationship with Scott and later accept his choice so he can pursue Ramona.

major_characters.name: Gideon Graves
major_characters.description: Record executive and Ramona's seventh, most powerful evil ex who sponsors Sex Bob-Omb.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Control Ramona and punish new suitors through manipulation and power.

original_full_tokens.plot_summary: In Beirut, undercover Lieutenant Frank Drebin (Leslie Nielsen) infiltrates a meeting of international villains posing as a waiter, beats them up and foils their plans. Back in Los Angeles, detective Nordberg (O.J. Simpson) attempts to bust a heroin deal at the docks linked to wealthy developer Vincent Ludwig (Ricardo Montalban) and is shot by Ludwig’s henchmen; Nordberg survives but is hospitalized. Captain Ed Hocken (George Kennedy) briefs Drebin and gives him 24 hours to clear Nordberg’s name amid police suspicion Nordberg was corrupt. Drebin consults police scientist Ted Olsen, who demonstrates a knockout cuff-link dart. Drebin visits Ludwig’s office, learns Ludwig is a respected developer, and inadvertently reveals Nordberg is alive, prompting an assassination attempt on Nordberg by a hypnotized physician; Drebin pursues the assassin in a teen-driving-school car through Los Angeles until the car crashes spectacularly. Drebin meets Ludwig’s assistant Jane Spencer (Priscilla Presley) and falls for her; Jane is unaware of Ludwig’s crimes. Pahpshmir (an intermediary) meets Ludwig and reveals Ludwig’s plan to sell a $20 million assassination contract on Queen Elizabeth II; Ludwig demonstrates a post-hypnotic trigger delivered via a beeper that can turn anyone into an unwitting assassin. Drebin breaks into Ludwig’s office to find evidence, accidentally sets it on fire, and later confronts Ludwig at a reception where Ludwig appears to try to assassinate the Queen; Drebin’s intervention gets him removed from Police Squad. While the Queen visits Los Angeles to attend a California Angels vs. Seattle Mariners baseball game, Jane tells Drebin that Ludwig has brainwashed an Angels right fielder—Reggie Jackson—to be the assassin using the beeper trigger. Drebin, hiding from police orders to arrest him, disguises himself as opera singer Enrico Palazzo and then secures the home-plate umpire position to search players for weapons and delay the game. Ludwig activates Jackson with the beeper; Jackson attempts to shoot the Queen during a bench-clearing brawl but Drebin fires Olsen’s cuff-link dart to incapacitate Jackson and instead hits a spectator; a fat woman falls onto Jackson and knocks him out, saving the Queen. Ludwig kidnaps Jane and takes her to the top of the stadium at gunpoint. Drebin reaches the top; he shoots Ludwig with the cuff-link dart, Ludwig staggers and falls over the stadium railing and lands under the marching band, which triggers Ludwig’s beeper and—through a post-hypnotic link—compels Jane to try to shoot Drebin. Drebin breaks Jane’s hypnosis by professing love, proposing with an engagement ring, and reverse-psychology; Jane snaps out of the suggestion and accepts. The stadium scoreboard broadcasts Drebin’s speech, prompting mass reconciliation among players and fans. Mayor Barkley reinstates Drebin to Police Squad; Nordberg congratulates him, but when Drebin pats Nordberg he accidentally sends him tumbling over the railing in a final gag.
original_full_tokens.setting: 1980s Los Angeles (with opening in Beirut)

major_characters.name: Frank Drebin
major_characters.description: Clumsy lieutenant of Police Squad! assigned to investigate Nordberg’s shooting and protect the Queen.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Clear Nordberg’s name, stop the assassination plot, and protect the Queen while pursuing Jane out of affection.

major_characters.name: Vincent Ludwig
major_characters.description: Wealthy developer and criminal mastermind who organizes assassination-for-hire schemes.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Assassinate Queen Elizabeth II for $20 million using post-hypnotic mind control to advance his power and profit.

major_characters.name: Jane Spencer
major_characters.description: Ludwig’s assistant who is initially unaware of his crimes and becomes Drebin’s love interest and ally.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Help Drebin when she learns Ludwig’s plot and survive Ludwig’s coercion, ultimately accepting Drebin’s proposal.

major_characters.name: Nordberg
major_characters.description: Drebin’s partner, wounded in Ludwig’s drug operation bust and hospitalized under suspicion of corruption.
major_characters.primary_motivations: Prove his innocence and recover from his injuries so he can return to duty.
